v.t: Zhu Wen seizes control of the court, Emperor Zhaozong becomes a puppet
v.s: Tang jiedushi (military governor) Zhu Wen (Zhu Quanzhong) gradually gained control over the Central Plains after suppressing the Huang Chao Rebellion. In 901, Zhu Wen led his army into Chang'an, killed the eunuchs, and took Emperor Zhaozong hostage, forcing him to move the capital to Luoyang. The emperor became a puppet, and the central authority of the Tang dynasty existed in name only. Zhu Wen later assassinated Emperor Zhaozong in 904 and installed Emperor Ai, paving the way for his eventual usurpation of the Tang.

v.t: Yelu Abaoji becomes Yilijin of the Diela tribe of the Khitans
v.s: Yelu Abaoji, chief of the Diela tribe of the Khitans, was elected as their Yilijin (military leader) for his bravery and skill in battle. He then led campaigns against neighboring tribes such as the Shiwei and Wugu, capturing many people and livestock, thus laying a military foundation for the rise of the Khitans. Over the next decade, Abaoji gradually unified the eight Khitan tribes, and in 916 formally proclaimed himself emperor, establishing the Liao dynasty.

v.t: The Maya city-state of Chichen Itza reaches its zenith
v.s: The Maya city-state of Chichen Itza in the northern Yucatán Peninsula entered a flourishing period under the influence of Toltec culture. Its rulers began constructing large structures such as the Kukulcán Pyramid (El Castillo), blending Maya astronomical calendars with Toltec military traditions. Chichen Itza gradually became the political and religious center of Postclassic Maya civilization, controlling trade networks across the northern peninsula.

v.t: First stone castles emerge in Western Europe
v.s: To defend against incursions by Magyars, Vikings, and Muslims, lords across Western Europe began converting wooden fortifications into stone castles. The Langeais Castle in the Loire Valley of France and Meersburg Castle in Germany are representative of this period. Stone walls could be up to three meters thick, complemented by towers and moats, greatly enhancing defensive capabilities. The spread of castles changed the nature of medieval warfare and consolidated feudal lords' regional control.

v.t: Zhu Wen besieges Fengxiang, seizes Emperor Zhaozong
v.s: Emperor Zhaozong was taken hostage by the eunuch Han Quanhui to Li Maozhen, the jiedushi of Fengxiang. Zhu Wen, under the pretext of "clearing the side of the emperor," led seventy thousand troops to besiege Fengxiang. When food inside the city ran out, Li Maozhen was forced to kill the eunuchs and hand over the emperor. Zhu Wen then brought the emperor back to Chang'an, fully seizing control of the court. This was a key step in Zhu Wen's usurpation of the Tang.

v.t: Kingdom of Croatia established
v.s: Duke Tomislav of Croatia, with the support of Pope John X, unified the Slavic tribes of the Pannonian Plain and the Dalmatian coast, defeating an invasion by Tsar Simeon I of Bulgaria. Tomislav proclaimed himself "King of Croatia," becoming the first Slavic monarch recognized by the pope. The kingdom's territory covered most of modern-day Croatia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, and the coastal areas of Montenegro.

v.t: Yang Xingmi establishes the Wu Kingdom
v.s: After repelling a southern invasion by Zhu Wen, Yang Xingmi, jiedushi of Huainan, was granted the title of Prince of Wu and established the Wu Kingdom (historically known as Yang Wu) with its capital at Yangzhou. Yang adopted a policy of rest and development, building irrigation works and promoting the tea and salt trades, sparing the Jianghuai region from severe devastation during the late Tang turmoil. The Wu Kingdom laid the foundation for the later Southern Tang dynasty.

v.t: Maturity of the manorial economy in Western Europe
v.s: By the early 10th century, the feudal manorial economy in Western Europe had largely taken shape. A manor was divided into the lord's demesne and the peasants' holdings; serfs were required to work for the lord three or four days a week without pay and pay rents in kind. The three-field rotation system became widespread west of the Rhine, increasing grain yields. Manors were largely self-sufficient, relying on external trade only for a few goods such as iron and salt.

v.t: Zhu Wen massacres all eunuchs, ending eunuch domination of the Tang court
v.s: After taking control of Emperor Zhaozong, Zhu Wen ordered a massacre of all eunuchs. All but thirty young ones left for menial tasks were beheaded in Luoyang, ending more than 150 years of eunuch domination at the Tang court. However, Zhu Wen's motive was not loyalty to the dynasty but to clear obstacles to his own usurpation.

v.t: Rise of Swahili city-states in East Africa
v.s: Along the Indian Ocean coast from Somalia to Mozambique, a number of Swahili city-states began to flourish. Kilwa, Mombasa, Zanzibar, and other places saw a fusion of Arab-Persian immigrants with Bantu peoples, creating Swahili culture. These city-states exported gold, ivory, and slaves, and imported porcelain, textiles, and glass beads, becoming key nodes in the Indian Ocean trade network.

v.t: The Duchy of Normandy consolidates its territory
v.s: Richard II, Duke of Normandy, descendant of the Viking leader Rollo, quelled a rebellion by his nobles and expanded the duchy's borders to the lower Seine and the Cotentin Peninsula. He introduced Frankish feudal institutions, encouraged conversion to Christianity, while maintaining Norse military traditions. Normandy became the most powerful semi-independent duchy in France, building strength for the later conquest of England.

v.t: Wang Jian, founder of Later Shu, captures Eastern Sichuan
v.s: Wang Jian, jiedushi of Xichuan, defeated Gu Yanhui, jiedushi of Dongchuan, and annexed Zizhou, bringing most of central and eastern Sichuan under his control. He was then granted the title of Prince of Shu by Emperor Zhaozong, paving the way for him to found the Former Shu in 907. He was the first person to establish a separatist kingdom in Sichuan during the late Tang and Five Dynasties period.

v.t: Zhu Wen murders Emperor Zhaozong, enthrones Emperor Ai
v.s: Zhu Wen forced Emperor Zhaozong to move the capital from Chang'an to Luoyang, then had his agent Jiang Xuanhui and others murder the emperor while he was drunk. He was only thirty-eight years old. Zhu Wen then installed Zhaozong's thirteenth son, Li Zhu, as Emperor Ai of Tang. The Tang dynasty existed in name only, entering its final three-year countdown.

v.t: The Arab Translation Movement nears its end
v.s: The House of Wisdom in Baghdad, capital of the Abbasid Caliphate, had for over a century translated most major Greek, Persian, and Indian works on philosophy, mathematics, medicine, and astronomy into Arabic. Scholars then built on these foundations with original research, producing masters like al-Khwarizmi and al-Razi. By 904 the translation movement was largely complete, and the Golden Age of Islamic science was at its peak.

v.t: Byzantine Macedonian dynasty expands
v.s: In the final years of Emperor Leo VI, his general Andronikos Doukas defeated Arab forces in Mesopotamia and recaptured border forts such as Martyropolis. Meanwhile, the Byzantine navy cleared the Aegean of Cretan pirates, restoring shipping security in the eastern Mediterranean. The military revival of the Macedonian dynasty laid the groundwork for Basil II's later conquest of Bulgaria.

v.t: Flourishing gold trade on Saharan routes
v.s: The Ghana Empire controlled the trans-Saharan trade routes from West Africa to North Africa. Camel caravans carried gold and ivory from the south to Morocco and Egypt, returning with salt, copper, and cloth. Around 904, Awdaghust and Timbuktu became major trading centers. The Saharan trade brought immense wealth to the Ghana Empire, leading Arab geographers to call it the "Land of Gold."

v.t: The Baima驿 Incident: Zhu Wen massacres court officials
v.s: To eliminate obstacles to his usurpation, Zhu Wen, following the advice of his strategist Li Zhen, gathered more than thirty court officials who opposed him, including Pei Shu and Dugu Sun, at Baima Post (present-day Hua County, Henan) and had them all killed. Their bodies were thrown into the Yellow River. Li Zhen remarked that these "pure stream" bureaucrats should be thrown into the "turbid current." The last stratum of Tang officials loyal to the Li family was completely destroyed.

v.t: Christianity formally introduced to Northern Europe
v.s: King Harald Bluetooth of Denmark erected the famous Jelling Rune Stone in Jutland, inscribed with the words: "King Harald made the Danes Christian." He forced the nobility to accept baptism and built the first wooden church of the Viking Age. Norway and Sweden subsequently converted as well. Northern Europe thus left its native beliefs and joined Latin Christendom.

v.t: Feudal hierarchy solidifies in Europe
v.s: The West Frankish kingdom issued a series of decrees clearly defining the feudal hierarchy: the king as supreme lord, followed in descending order by dukes, counts, viscounts, and knights. Lords at each level held judicial, tax-collecting, and conscription rights over their vassals. Vassals had to perform homage and swear fealty to their lords. This system completely fragmented political power in Europe.

v.t: Persian philosopher al-Farabi completes his commentary on Aristotle
v.s: Al-Farabi, born in Central Asia, completed his work "The Philosophy of Aristotle" in Baghdad, systematically commenting on Aristotle's logic, metaphysics, and ethics. He also proposed a model for an ideal city, deeply influenced by Plato's Republic. Al-Farabi is known as the "Second Teacher" (the first being Aristotle) in the Islamic world.

v.t: Zhu Wen completes preparations for usurping the Tang
v.s: One year before forcing Emperor Ai to "abdicate" in his favor, Zhu Wen was granted the nine bestowments, named Prince of Wei, and put his confidants in control of the imperial guards and key local posts. Except for a few autonomous governors like Li Keyong and Li Maozhen, most of the Tang territory had already submitted to Zhu Wen. He also ordered Chancellor Liu Can to compile the Book of Liang to create favorable public opinion for his accession.

v.t: Queen Kim of Silla converts to Buddhism
v.s: Amid intensifying internal strife among the Silla royal family, Queen Kim, wife of the last king, King Gyeongsun, became a nun at Bulguksa Temple in Gyeongju. She donated large amounts of gold and silver to the temple, sponsoring the carving of Buddhist scriptures. The art of Silla Buddhism reached its peak during this period, with masterpieces like the octagonal stone pagoda at Seokguram and the Blue and White Clouds Bridges at Bulguksa. But corruption in the royal house had already lost the people's hearts.

v.t: Byzantine navy rebuilds rapid-strike fleet
v.s: Byzantine Emperor Leo VI ordered the construction of fifty dromon warships in the Golden Horn shipyards of Constantinople. These fast ships were equipped with Greek fire projectors and three banks of oars, giving them much greater maneuverability than Arab vessels. The Byzantine fleet subsequently launched a surprise attack on the Arab pirates based on Crete, destroying their port facilities.

v.t: Peasant uprising in Jukju, Silla
v.s: In the late Silla period, land concentration was severe and taxes were heavy. Peasants in Jukju (present-day North Chungcheong Province, South Korea), led by a chief named Gi Seon, rose in revolt, calling for "from each according to his ability, to each according to his needs." The rebels briefly captured Chungju before being crushed by allied aristocratic forces. The uprising foreshadowed the imminent fall of Silla.

d.u: Emperor Taizu of Later Liang (Zhu Wen/Zhu Quanzhong)

v.t: Zhu Wen usurps the Tang, establishes Later Liang, Tang dynasty falls
v.s: Zhu Wen forced Emperor Ai of Tang to abdicate and proclaimed himself emperor in Kaifeng with the dynasty name Liang, known historically as Later Liang. The Tang dynasty, founded in 618, had ruled through twenty emperors and lasted 289 years before its final fall. China entered the great fragmentation period of the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms. Zhu Wen is known as Emperor Taizu of Later Liang.

v.t: Yelu Abaoji unites the eight Khitan tribes, proclaimed Khan
v.s: Yelu Abaoji, having defeated the chiefs of the other seven tribes from his base in the Diela tribe, convened a tribal assembly and was elected common Khan of the eight Khitan tribes. He built a capital called Xilou (later renamed Shangjing Linhuangfu) and established the Northern and Southern Administration, governing Khitans and Han Chinese separately. This was a crucial step in the transition of the Khitan from a tribal confederation to a state; nine years later he would formally proclaim the Liao dynasty.

v.t: Qu Chengjun of Vietnam establishes autonomy, beginning of Vietnamese independence era
v.s: After the fall of the Tang dynasty, Qu Chengjun, the jiedushi of Jinghai Circuit (the former Tang Protectorate of Annan), proclaimed himself "Jiedushi of Annan" and no longer submitted to central Chinese authority. Although he nominally acknowledged Later Liang as suzerain, in practice he ruled independently. The Qu regime paved the way for the formal establishment of the Đinh dynasty. Vietnamese historiography regards this as the beginning of Vietnam's era of autonomy.

v.t: Magyars raid East Francia
v.s: Magyar horsemen set out from the Pannonian Plain, advanced up the Danube, and plundered Bavaria and Swabia. They burned Augsburg, Regensburg, and other cities, and captured hundreds of clergy and nobles for ransom. Louis the Child, king of East Francia, was powerless to resist and had to pay tribute for a truce. Magyar incursions would continue until their defeat at the Battle of Lechfeld in 955.

d.u: Emperor Taizu of Later Liang (Zhu Wen)

v.t: Li Keyong dies; Li Cunxu succeeds as Prince of Jin
v.s: Li Keyong, Prince of Jin, died of illness in Taiyuan and was succeeded by his son Li Cunxu, only twenty-three years old. On his deathbed, Li Keyong gave his son three arrows, representing his enmity with Zhu Wen of Later Liang, Liu Rengong of Hebei, and Yelu Abaoji of the Khitans. Li Cunxu enshrined the arrows in his ancestral temple, taking one with each campaign to inspire his troops. He later destroyed Later Liang and founded the Later Tang dynasty.

v.t: Later Liang campaign against Luzhou fails; Jin army wins great victory
v.s: Zhu Wen sent a large army to besiege Luzhou (present-day Changzhi, Shanxi) in an attempt to cut off the Jin army's communications with Hedong. Li Cunxu personally led elite troops across the Taihang Mountains and launched a heavy-fog surprise attack on the Later Liang camp. The Liang forces, caught completely off guard, were routed and suffered over ten thousand casualties. After this battle, Later Liang was unable to mount another northern offensive, and the Jin base in Hedong was secured.

v.t: Gung Ye of Goryeo establishes Taebong kingdom
v.s: Gung Ye, a northern Silla general, rebelled against Silla and established a state first called Later Goguryeo and later renamed Taebong. He made Cheorwon (in present-day Gangwon Province) his capital, claimed to be a living incarnation of Maitreya Buddha, and ruled by harsh laws. Gung Ye became so tyrannical that his general Wang Geon overthrew him in 918 and established the Goryeo dynasty.

v.t: Fatimid Egypt conquers Cyrenaica
v.s: Fatimid caliph Ubayd Allah al-Mahdi sent his army eastward from Tunisia, defeating the remnants of the Aghlabid dynasty, vassals of the Abbasids, and occupied Cyrenaica in Libya. The Fatimid sphere of influence expanded to the south-central Mediterranean, setting the stage for their conquest of Egypt in 969.

d.u: Emperor Taizu of Later Liang (Zhu Wen)

v.t: Later Liang moves capital to Luoyang
v.s: Zhu Wen moved the capital from Kaifeng to Luoyang to be closer to the old Tang capital, Chang'an, and to emphasize his legitimacy. He conscripted tens of thousands of laborers to restore the palaces of Luoyang at enormous expense. However, this action also deepened the conflict between Liang and the Hebei and Hedong warlords, since Luoyang was more vulnerable to attack by the Jin army.

v.t: Fatimid caliph founds new capital al-Mahdiyya in Tunisia
v.s: Fatimid caliph Ubayd Allah al-Mahdi chose a location on the eastern coast of Tunisia to build a new capital called al-Mahdiyya ("City of the Mahdi"). The city, protected by the sea on three sides, had strong walls and a port. From this base, the Fatimids conquered Morocco to the west and later expanded into Egypt.

v.t: Umayyad emir in Córdoba proclaims caliphate
v.s: Abd al-Rahman III, the Umayyad emir of Spain, proclaimed himself caliph to counter the Fatimids and the Abbasid Caliphate in Baghdad, establishing the Caliphate of Córdoba. Córdoba became one of Europe's largest and most prosperous cities, boasting seventy libraries and nine hundred public baths, marking the golden age of Islamic Spain.

v.t: Emperor Daigo of Japan initiates the Engi Reforms
v.s: After assuming personal rule, Emperor Daigo of Japan appointed Fujiwara no Tadahira as chancellor (kampaku) and reformed the legal code, streamlining local administration. He compiled the Engi Shiki, revising land taxation and household registration, and prohibited illegal expansion of private estates, strengthening the central treasury. Culturally, this period is known as the "Engi Golden Age," and the anthology of Chinese poetry and waka, the Kokin Wakashū, was compiled.

v.t: Cluniac Reforms begin
v.s: Duke William I of Aquitaine founded the Abbey of Cluny in Burgundy, placing it directly under papal protection, free from control by any secular lord or bishop. The abbey emphasized strict adherence to the Rule of Saint Benedict, opposing simony and clerical corruption. This reform movement spread rapidly, becoming the Cluniac movement of the 10th–11th centuries and greatly strengthening papal authority.

v.t: Former Shu emperor Wang Jian builds the outer wall of Chengdu
v.s: Wang Jian, emperor of Former Shu, conscripted 100,000 laborers to build an outer wall forty-two li in circumference around Chengdu, making the city one of the most heavily fortified in the Five Dynasties period. The walls featured barbicans, corner towers, and projecting battlements, with a moat fed by rivers. Wang Jian also expanded the imperial palace, restoring to Chengdu some of the prosperity of the old Tang capital, Chang'an.

v.t: Danish Vikings raid the east coast of England
v.s: A Danish Viking fleet under the command of chief Erik the Red sailed down the east coast of England, sacking York, Lincoln, and Norwich. They looted gold and silver vessels from churches and monasteries and captured people for the slave trade. Edward the Elder, king of the Anglo-Saxons, organized a defense but could not eliminate the Viking threat.

v.t: Persian poet Rudaki composes Kalila wa Dimna
v.s: Rudaki, a court poet of the Samanid Empire, translated and adapted the Indian collection of fables known as the Panchatantra into Persian verse as Kalila wa Dimna. He used simple Persian and new poetic meters, contributing to the revival of Persian literature. Rudaki is celebrated as the "Father of Persian Poetry."

d.u: Emperor Taizu of Later Liang (Zhu Wen)

v.t: Duchy of Normandy formally established
v.s: Charles III (the Simple), king of West Francia, concluded the Treaty of Saint-Clair-sur-Epte with the Viking leader Rollo, granting him the lands from the Seine estuary to Rouen to establish the Duchy of Normandy. Rollo and his men converted to Christianity and swore fealty to the French king. Normandy thereupon became one of the most powerful fiefs in France.

v.t: Gao Jixing establishes the Jingnan regime
v.s: Later Liang appointed Gao Jixing as jiedushi of Jingnan, with his seat at Jiangling (present-day Jingzhou, Hubei). Taking advantage of the chaos in the south, Gao gradually broke free from Later Liang's control, becoming an independent power. Although his Jingnan regime controlled only three prefectures—the smallest of the Ten Kingdoms—it survived for forty years by relying on commercial taxes and tribute trade.

v.t: Later Liang casts Kaiping Tongbao coins
v.s: Emperor Taizu of Later Liang ordered the minting of "Kaiping Tongbao" copper coins, the first official currency of the Five Dynasties period. However, copper resources were scarce, so few were minted, and the populace continued to use old Tang coins and cloth as mediums of exchange.

v.t: Magyars raid Byzantine Thrace
v.s: Magyar horsemen crossed the Danube and plundered Philippopolis (modern Plovdiv, Bulgaria) in Byzantine Thrace. Emperor Leo VI hastily recalled troops from the Arab front and paid gold to ransom captives. The Byzantines were forced to strengthen the Danube frontier, building dozens of new border fortresses.

d.u: Emperor Taizu of Later Liang (Zhu Wen)

v.t: Zhu Wen murdered by his son Zhu Yougui
v.s: When Emperor Taizu of Later Liang fell gravely ill, he intended to pass the throne to his adopted son Zhu Youwen. His biological son Zhu Yougui, taking advantage of his attendance at the emperor's sickbed, led his personal guards into the bedchamber and stabbed Zhu Wen to death, then wrapped the body in a blanket and buried it. He then forged an edict and proclaimed himself emperor. Zhu Wen was a cruel and lecherous man who had forced himself upon his daughters-in-law, and he eventually met his end at the hands of his own son.

v.t: Fatimids capture Alexandria
v.s: The Fatimid navy sailed from al-Mahdiyya and took advantage of internal strife among the Abbasid governor of Egypt to capture Alexandria, the Mediterranean gateway to Egypt. This gave the Fatimids a beachhead in Egypt, but the real conquest of the country would not occur until 969. This occupation lasted only a few months before Abbasid forces retook the city.

v.t: Henry the Fowler is crowned Duke of Saxony
v.s: After the death of Louis the Child, king of East Francia, the Saxon duke Henry the Fowler was elected king by the nobles (though he would not be formally crowned until 919). Henry began reorganizing the German army, training heavy cavalry to fight the Magyars, and building castles along the frontier. He was the father of Otto the Great, founder of the Holy Roman Empire.

v.t: Ki no Tsurayuki writes the Tosa Diary
v.s: The Japanese noble Ki no Tsurayuki, after completing his term as governor of Tosa Province (present-day Kōchi Prefecture) and on his way back to Kyoto, wrote a diary in the voice of a woman, describing his journey and the grief of losing his daughter. This is the first diary work in Japanese literature and inaugurated the tradition of kana literature.

d.u: Emperor Modi of Later Liang (Zhu Youzhen)

v.t: Zhu Youzhen kills his brother and seizes the throne; civil war in Later Liang
v.s: After Zhu Yougui had murdered his father and proclaimed himself emperor, his brother Zhu Youzhen conspired with imperial guard officers to attack the palace in Luoyang and kill Zhu Yougui. Zhu Youzhen then succeeded him, becoming the last emperor of Later Liang. The two successive coups greatly weakened Later Liang, giving an opportunity to Li Cunxu, the Prince of Jin.

v.t: Byzantine Macedonian dynasty defeats Bulgaria
v.s: During the brief reign of Byzantine Emperor Alexander (brother of Leo VI), his generals captured the Bulgarian border fortress of Preslav, forcing Tsar Simeon I to sue for peace. The Byzantines recovered lost territory in northern Thrace and regained control of Black Sea trade.

v.t: Nanzhao falls; precursor of the Dali Kingdom emerges
v.s: Zheng Maisi, a powerful minister in Nanzhao, staged a coup, killing the last Nanzhao king, Shunhuazhen, and more than eight hundred members of the royal family, ending the Nanzhao kingdom. Zheng Maisi established the Great Changhe Kingdom, but internal contradictions were severe. More than thirty years later, Duan Siping would replace it and found the Dali Kingdom.

v.t: Igor I of Kievan Rus' leads an expedition against Constantinople
v.s: Grand Prince Igor I of Kiev led a fleet of several thousand dugouts down the Dnieper into the Black Sea in an attempt to plunder the Byzantine capital. The Byzantine navy used Greek fire to burn the Rus' boats, and Igor was forced to retreat in panic. This was the last major Rus' attack on Byzantium; thereafter their relations turned to trade and marriage alliances.

v.t: Later Liang's last emperor Zhu Youzhen kills the powerful minister Li Zhen
v.s: To purge his former allies in the coup that killed his brother, Emperor Modi of Later Liang executed the chancellor Li Zhen on charges of "abusing power and creating chaos." He also demoted a number of old officials and appointed literati such as Jing Xiang and Li Qi to high office. However, Modi was suspicious and jealous, exacerbating internal disunity.

v.t: Tsar Simeon I of Bulgaria captures Adrianople
v.s: Taking advantage of a Byzantine civil war, Tsar Simeon I of the First Bulgarian Empire led his army south, besieging and capturing the important Thracian city of Adrianople (modern Edirne). He captured the Byzantine governor of the city and forced the Byzantine regent, Empress Zoe, to recognize Bulgarian sovereignty over northern parts of the Balkans.

v.t: Former Shu and Southern Han establish friendly relations to resist Later Liang
v.s: Wang Jian, emperor of Former Shu, and Liu Yan, ruler of Southern Han, formed an alliance to jointly resist the edicts of Later Liang. Envoys traveled back and forth via the Lingqu Canal, exchanging silk, tea, and pearls. This was a diplomatic league among the Ten Kingdoms against the Central Plains dynasty.

v.t: Compilation of the Engi Shiki completed in Japan
v.s: Emperor Daigo of Japan ordered the completion of the Engi Shiki in fifty volumes, a systematic compilation of statutes, regulations, official ranks, rituals, taxes, and other systems. This work became the standard for court administration in the mid-Heian period and had a lasting impact.

d.u: Emperor Modi of Later Liang (Zhu Youzhen)

v.t: Li Cunxu, Prince of Jin, battles Later Liang at Yangliu
v.s: Li Cunxu personally led his army across the Yellow River and confronted the Later Liang forces at Yangliu (present-day Dong'e County, Shandong). The Liang general Wang Yanzhang tried to block the Jin retreat with iron chains across the river. Li Cunxu launched a night attack on the Liang camp, burning their ships and routing the Liang army. After this victory, the Jin occupied most of Shandong, and the Later Liang line of defense was pushed south of the Yellow River.

v.t: The Ghana Empire reaches its zenith
v.s: The Ghana Empire in West Africa, under King Koniba Touni, reached its peak. The empire controlled the gold-producing regions of the upper Senegal and Niger rivers and fielded an army of 200,000, including 40,000 archers. The Arab geographer al-Ya'qubi recorded that the king of Ghana possessed a "golden throne" and vast numbers of gold coins.

v.t: Fatimids invade Sicily
v.s: The Fatimid navy sailed from Tunisia and captured Palermo, Trapani, and other cities in western Sicily. Byzantine forces withdrew to Messina. The Fatimids then established their governorate for Sicily in Palermo, beginning decades of Fatimid rule over the island.

v.t: Worship of Odin continues in Iceland
v.s: Most of Iceland's inhabitants were immigrants fleeing the oppression of Harald Fairhair of Norway. They still worshiped the Norse gods Odin (Wodan), Thor, and others. Annual sacrifices were held, and the law assembly, the Thing, was held in front of a temple. The Christian church had not yet been established in Iceland.

d.u: Emperor Modi of Later Liang (Zhu Youzhen)

v.t: Yelu Abaoji proclaims himself emperor, establishes Liao dynasty
v.s: Yelu Abaoji formally proclaimed himself emperor at Longhua Prefecture (modern Naiman Banner, Inner Mongolia), taking the dynastic name of "Khitan" (later changed to "Liao"), and styled himself the Great Sage Great Bright Heavenly Emperor, with the reign name Shence. He established a bureaucracy modeled on Chinese systems and created the Khitan large script and the Khitan small script. The Liao dynasty became an empire in the north standing alongside the Five Dynasties and the Northern Song.

v.t: Li Cunxu captures Weizhou
v.s: Li Cunxu captured Weizhou (present-day Daming, Hebei) from Later Liang, taking the Liang general Liu Xun prisoner. The Weibo circuit surrendered to the Jin. Weizhou was the seat of the Tianxiong Army, which possessed an elite corps known as the "Silver Spear and Halberd Army." Li Cunxu incorporated this unit into his forces, greatly strengthening his military power and laying the foundation for his eventual destruction of Liang.

v.t: The Lighthouse of Alexandria collapses in an earthquake
v.s: A powerful earthquake struck the Mediterranean coast, causing the upper structure of the Lighthouse of Alexandria (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World) to collapse. The lighthouse gradually fell into ruin, and in 1480 the sultan used its stone to build a fortress.

v.t: Kingdom of Burgundy splits into Upper and Lower Burgundy
v.s: After the death of King Rudolf II of Burgundy, the kingdom was divided into Upper Burgundy (around Arles) and Lower Burgundy (Provence). The two were absorbed into the spheres of influence of the Holy Roman Empire and West Francia respectively, and Burgundy would never be reunited.

v.t: Liu Yan proclaims himself emperor in Guangzhou, establishing Southern Han
v.s: Liu Yan, jiedushi of Qinghai Circuit, proclaimed himself emperor in Guangzhou with the dynastic name Da Yue (later changed to Han), known historically as Southern Han. He renamed Guangzhou as Xingwang Prefecture, constructed palaces, and relied on eunuchs and palace women, enforcing harsh laws. The Southern Han kingdom ruled the Lingnan region for more than sixty years, achieving commercial prosperity but suffering from political corruption.

v.t: Emperor Taizu of Liao captures the capital of the Balhae kingdom at Fuyu Prefecture
v.s: Yelu Abaoji led the Khitan army eastward against the Balhae kingdom, capturing its western stronghold of Fuyu Prefecture (present-day Nong'an, Jilin). King Dae Inseon of Balhae was forced to move his capital to Sanggyeong Yongcheonbu (Ning'an, Heilongjiang). Khitan encroachment on Balhae had become irreversible.

v.t: Charles III of West Francia suppresses the rebellion in Lorraine
v.s: Charles III (the Simple), king of West Francia, marched into Lorraine to suppress a rebellion by local nobles against the Duchy of Normandy. He executed the rebel leaders and reaffirmed Lorraine as a territory of West Francia. This was one of the few military victories of Charles III's reign.

v.t: Quanzhou merchants reach Srivijaya for the first time
v.s: A merchant from Quanzhou, Fujian, named Wang Hou, sailed with the monsoon winds to the Srivijaya kingdom (also known as Palembang) on Sumatra. They exchanged Chinese porcelain and silk for local spices, pearls, and ivory. This marked the beginning of maritime trade between China and Southeast Asia in the Song and Yuan dynasties.

d.u: Emperor Modi of Later Liang (Zhu Youzhen)

v.t: Wang Geon establishes the Goryeo dynasty
v.s: Wang Geon, a general of the kingdom of Taebong, overthrew the tyrant Gung Ye and proclaimed himself king, changing the name of the kingdom to Goryeo and establishing the capital at Kaegyeong (modern Kaesong). He allied with local powerful families and promoted Buddhism as the state religion. Wang Geon unified the Later Three Kingdoms, and the Goryeo dynasty would last nearly five hundred years.

v.t: Wang Jian of Former Shu proclaims himself emperor
v.s: Wang Jian of Former Shu proclaimed himself emperor in Chengdu, with the dynastic name Da Shu (historically known as Former Shu). He appointed Wei Zhuang, a former Tang official, as his chancellor, reduced taxes and forced labor, and encouraged agriculture, making Shu one of the few prosperous regions during the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period.

v.t: Emperor Taizu of Liao builds the upper capital Linhuangfu
v.s: Yelu Abaoji built a royal capital on the north bank of the Huang River (present-day Xilamulun River), later called Shangjing Linhuangfu. The city was divided into a northern city, the "imperial city" where Khitans lived, and a southern city, the "Han city" where Han Chinese and merchants lived. This was the first fixed capital built by the Khitans.

v.t: The Tafsir al-Tabari, a commentary on the Qur'an, is completed
v.s: The Persian scholar Muhammad ibn Jarir al-Tabari completed his thirty-volume comprehensive commentary on the Qur'an, known as the Tafsir al-Tabari. It gathered earlier hadiths and scholarly interpretations of the Qur'anic verses and is one of the most authoritative Sunni tafsirs.

d.u: Emperor Modi of Later Liang (Zhu Youzhen)

v.t: Yang Longyan proclaims himself King of Wu, formally establishing the Wu Kingdom
v.s: Yang Longyan, jiedushi of Huainan, under pressure from his officials, proclaimed himself King of Wu, changing the reign title to Wuyi and making Jiangdu (Yangzhou) his capital. The Wu Kingdom was the first of the Ten Kingdoms to formally adopt the title of king. Although Yang Longyan had ascended the throne, real power remained in the hands of the powerful minister Xu Wen.

v.t: Henry I is crowned King of East Francia
v.s: Henry I, Duke of Saxony, was elected King of East Francia by the nobles at Fritzlar and formally crowned. He refused the church's anointing ritual, signifying that the king's authority came from the election of the nobility. Henry began building a cavalry force and constructing frontier castles to defend against the Magyars.

v.t: Byzantine Emperor Romanos I Lecapenus is crowned
v.s: The naval commander Romanos I Lecapenus, taking advantage of the youth of Constantine VII, proclaimed himself co-emperor. He effectively wielded Byzantine power, promulgating land laws and military reforms to limit the accumulation of military land by large landowners.

v.t: Japan compiles the Engi Shinsen
v.s: Emperor Daigo ordered the collection of folk tales and legends into the twenty-volume Engi Shinsen. This work blends Buddhist karma stories with Shinto legends and is a major source for later collections such as the Konjaku Monogatari.

d.u: Emperor Modi of Later Liang (Zhu Youzhen)

v.t: Yelu Abaoji creates the Khitan large script
v.s: Emperor Taizu of Liao ordered Yelu Tulubu and others to create a Khitan large script of about three thousand characters, based on the shape of Chinese characters. This ideographic script was the beginning of a written language for the Khitan people. It was promulgated on stone tablets that same year.

v.t: Karakhanids conquer the Samanid dynasty
v.s: The Karakhanid dynasty of Central Asia, under Satuk Bughra Khan, captured Bukhara, the capital of the Samanid dynasty. The last Samanid king went into exile. The Karakhanids converted to Islam and became the most powerful state in Central Asia.

v.t: Srivijaya declines due to civil strife
v.s: The Srivijaya empire on Sumatra fell into rapid decline due to a civil war among princes fighting over the throne. Its vassals, Mataram (Java) and Kedah (Malay Peninsula), took the opportunity to break away, and Srivijaya lost its monopoly over maritime trade.

v.t: Battle of the Fat Cow: Henry I defeats the Magyars
v.s: King Henry I of East Francia engaged the Magyar cavalry on the Saale River. He intentionally threw a fat cow toward the Magyar camp, and while they were distracted by feasting on it, he launched a surprise attack, routing the Magyars. This battle temporarily halted Magyar incursions.

d.u: Emperor Modi of Later Liang (Zhu Youzhen)

v.t: Yelu Abaoji builds the imperial capital Shangjing
v.s: Emperor Taizu of Liao ordered the Han Chinese advisor Kang Muji to supervise the construction of an imperial capital on the steppe (later known as Shangjing Linhuangfu). This was the first walled capital city built by the Khitans, signifying the Liao dynasty's transition from a nomadic to a more urbanized state.

v.t: The Khitans attack Youzhou but are defeated by Li Cunxu
v.s: Yelu Abaoji led 300,000 Khitan cavalry south to besiege Youzhou (present-day Beijing). Li Cunxu, Prince of Jin, personally led his iron cavalry to the rescue, defeating the Khitan army under the walls of Youzhou and forcing Abaoji to withdraw north. This battle prevented Khitan power from penetrating deep into the Central Plains.

v.t: Al-Muqtadir becomes Abbasid caliph in Baghdad
v.s: Al-Muqtadir, only thirteen years old, was proclaimed the 18th caliph of the Abbasid dynasty by the nobles. He reigned for twenty-five years, but real power was held by bureaucrats and the commanders of the guard, and the caliph's authority steadily declined.

v.t: Cornish people rebel against the Kingdom of Wessex
v.s: The Cornish of southwestern Britain rose in rebellion against the rule of King Edward the Elder of Wessex. Edward sent his army to suppress the rebellion, incorporating Cornwall into Wessex and establishing a new marcher earldom.

v.t: Arab physician al-Razi dies
v.s: The Persian physician Muhammad ibn Zakariya al-Razi died in Baghdad. He authored the Comprehensive Book of Medicine and the Treatise on Smallpox and Measles, distinguishing between smallpox and measles, and established clinical teaching at the hospital in Baghdad. He is known as the "Galen of Islam."

v.t: Ibn Fadlan visits Volga Bulgaria
v.s: The Abbasid caliph sent a mission to Volga Bulgaria; the accompanying scribe, Ibn Fadlan, kept a detailed record of the journey, including the ship burial customs of the Vikings and their raw eating habits. His travelogue is an important source on early Nordic civilization.

v.t: The Maya build the Pyramid of the Magician at Uxmal
v.s: The Maya of the city-state of Uxmal began constructing the "Pyramid of the Magician" (also known as the Pyramid of the Dwarf). This pyramid features an elliptical base and rounded steps, standing forty meters high. Local legend says it was built overnight by a deity.

v.t: Southern Han captures Jiaozhou, Vietnam briefly annexed
v.s: Liu Yan, founder of Southern Han, sent his son Liu Hongcao with a combined land and naval force to attack Qu Chengmei, the jiedushi of Jinghai Circuit, capturing Jiaozhou (northern Vietnam). However, soon afterward a local strongman, Ngo Quyen, raised an army and defeated the Southern Han fleet in the Battle of Bach Dang River, restoring Vietnamese independence.

d.u: Emperor Zhuangzong of Later Tang (Li Cunxu)

v.t: Li Cunxu destroys Later Liang, establishes Later Tang
v.s: Li Cunxu, Prince of Jin, proclaimed himself emperor at Weizhou, with the dynastic name Tang (historically known as Later Tang), using the reign title Tongguang, which had been used by Emperor Zhaozong of Tang. He then sent a detachment led by Li Siyuan on a lightning march to Bianzhou (Kaifeng), where the last emperor of Later Liang, Zhu Youzhen, committed suicide. Later Liang fell. Li Cunxu declared himself the legitimate heir of the Tang dynasty and moved the capital to Luoyang.

v.t: Robert I of West Francia takes the throne
v.s: The West Frankish nobles elected Robert, Count of Paris, as king (Robert I), replacing Charles III of the Carolingian dynasty. Robert was an ancestor of the Capetian dynasty, and his accession marked the decline of Carolingian royal power.

v.t: Emperor Zhuangzong of Later Tang establishes his capital at Luoyang
v.s: After destroying Liang, Li Cunxu moved his capital from Jinyang (Taiyuan) to the old Later Liang capital of Luoyang, restoring the Tang palaces. He also had the remains of Emperor Taizu of Later Liang, Zhu Wen, exhumed from his tomb and exposed to the public, as punishment for the usurper.

v.t: The Althing of Iceland convenes
v.s: The chieftains of Iceland convened the Althing assembly in the Þingvellir rift valley to formulate common laws and settle disputes. The Althing is one of the world's oldest existing parliamentary institutions, marking the formation of a common Icelandic political community.

d.u: Emperor Zhuangzong of Later Tang (Li Cunxu)

v.t: Li Cunxu indulges court actors, government degenerates
v.s: After destroying Liang, Emperor Zhuangzong of Later Tang became addicted to theater and music, lavishing high office and rich rewards on his favorite actors, Jing Jin and Shi Yanqiong. The interference of these actors in government affairs aroused discontent among his generals, laying the seeds for the later Xingjiao Gate Rebellion.

v.t: Henry I defeats the Magyars on the Unstrut River
v.s: Henry I of East Francia set an ambush on the Unstrut River to intercept an invading Magyar cavalry force. He used heavy cavalry to charge the Magyar light horse, killing more than four thousand. After this battle the Magyars did not dare invade Germany for eleven years.

v.t: The Dayining kingdom is established in the former Nanzhao territory
v.s: The Yunnan noble Yang Ganzhen usurped the Great Changhe Kingdom and established the Dayining kingdom. But Yang was cruel and unpopular; he was overthrown six years later by Duan Siping, and the Dali Kingdom era was about to begin.

v.t: King Qian Liu of Wuyue builds a seawall at Qiantang
v.s: Qian Liu, king of Wuyue, built a sea-defending stone embankment in Hangzhou Bay to resist tidal erosion. He used the "bamboo cage stone embankment" method: bamboo baskets filled with stones were stacked in layers, creating a sturdy anti-flood structure. The farmlands along the Qiantang estuary were thus protected.

d.u: Emperor Zhuangzong of Later Tang (Li Cunxu)

v.t: Later Tang destroys Former Shu
v.s: Emperor Zhuangzong of Later Tang sent his son Li Jiji and the Commissioner of Military Affairs Guo Chongtao with 60,000 troops to attack Former Shu. The army crossed the Qin Mountains from Fengzhou (Feng County, Shaanxi), capturing Xingzhou, Lizhou, and Mianzhou in succession, and then advanced directly on Chengdu. Wang Yan, the last emperor of Former Shu, surrendered, and Former Shu fell. Later Tang gained sixty prefectures in Shu.

v.t: Yelu Abaoji campaigns west against the Turkic tribes
v.s: Emperor Taizu of Liao led his army west, conquering Turkic tribes such as the Karluks and Yagmas, extending the western border of the Liao dynasty to the Altai Mountains. Abaoji erected a stone stele to commemorate his victory on the return journey.

v.t: Liu Bin, later ruler of Southern Han, succeeds to the throne
v.s: Liu Yan, the founder of Southern Han, died and was succeeded by his son Liu Bin (known as the Martyr of Southern Han). Liu Bin was extremely dissolute and ignored state affairs, entrusting the government to eunuchs and palace ladies. Three years later his brother Liu Sheng killed him and seized the throne.

v.t: Byzantine Empire recruits the Varangian Guard
v.s: Emperor Romanos I of Byzantium formally organized mercenaries from Kievan Rus' and Scandinavia into the Varangian Guard, serving as the emperor's personal bodyguard. The Varangians were renowned for their loyalty and bravery and became an important military force at the Byzantine court.

d.u: Emperor Mingzong of Later Tang (Li Siyuan)

v.t: Xingjiao Gate Rebellion: Li Cunxu killed, Li Siyuan succeeds
v.s: Actors favored by Emperor Zhuangzong of Later Tang conspired with some imperial guards to mutiny and storm the palace. Li Cunxu was shot with a stream arrow and died at Xingjiao Gate. The old followers of Guo Chongtao promoted Li Siyuan (Zhuangzong's adopted elder brother) to the throne, becoming Emperor Mingzong of Later Tang. After his accession, Mingzong worked hard to improve governance and ended the practice of actors interfering in politics.

v.t: Yelu Abaoji dies; his second son Yelu Deguang succeeds
v.s: Emperor Taizu of Liao, Yelu Abaoji, died of illness at Fuyu Prefecture (present-day Nong'an, Jilin) on his way back from a campaign against the Balhae kingdom, at the age of fifty-five. Empress Dowager Shulü Ping acted as regent, deposing the crown prince Yelu Bei and elevating the second son, Yelu Deguang, to the throne as Emperor Taizong of Liao.

v.t: Balhae kingdom is conquered by the Khitans
v.s: Emperor Taizong of Liao continued the campaign against the Balhae kingdom after his accession. The last king of Balhae, Dae Inseon, surrendered, and the Balhae kingdom fell. The Khitans established the Dongdan (Eastern Khitan) Kingdom in its place, appointing the former crown prince Yelu Bei as its king. The 229-year-old Balhae kingdom came to an end.

v.t: The Engi Shiki is finally completed and promulgated in Japan
v.s: After two generations of effort under Emperor Daigo, the Engi Shiki in fifty volumes was finally completed and promulgated. This detailed code of administrative regulations covered state rituals, official posts, penalties, taxes, and more, and is a valuable historical source for the study of Heian period institutional history.

d.u: Emperor Mingzong of Later Tang (Li Siyuan)

v.t: Æthelstan unites England
v.s: Æthelstan, king of Wessex, defeated the Vikings of Northumbria and captured York. He then received the submission of the kings of Scotland and Wales, becoming the first Anglo-Saxon monarch to rule all of England. He styled himself "King of all Britain."

v.t: Goryeo unifies the Later Three Kingdoms of Korea
v.s: King Taejo of Goryeo, Wang Geon, personally led a campaign against Later Baekje, capturing its capital and causing the king to surrender. He also sent forces north to eliminate the remnants of Silla. Thus Goryeo unified the Later Three Kingdoms of the Korean peninsula, ending thirty years of war.

v.t: Later Tang and the Khitans make peace
v.s: Emperor Mingzong of Later Tang sent envoys to the Khitans to congratulate Yelu Deguang on his succession and present rich gifts. Yelu Deguang responded by sending his own envoys, and the two sides agreed to cease hostilities and not invade each other. For the next ten years the Song–Liao border was largely peaceful.

v.t: Arab geographer al-Mas'udi completes The Meadows of Gold
v.s: The Arab scholar al-Mas'udi, after years of travel in Asia and Africa, completed his great historical and geographical work, The Meadows of Gold and Mines of Gems, describing the customs, history, and geography of India, Persia, Egypt, East Africa, and other regions. It is an important source for the history of the 10th century.

d.u: Emperor Mingzong of Later Tang (Li Siyuan)

v.t: King Qian Liu of Wuyue builds the inner city of Hangzhou
v.s: Qian Liu, king of Wuyue, built a new palace and inner city at the foot of Phoenix Mountain in Hangzhou, turning the city into the "leading prefecture of the southeast." He dredged West Lake and built sea walls, making Hangzhou an economic and cultural center of the Jiangnan region.

v.t: Emperor Taizong of Liao campaigns south, battles Later Tang at Youzhou
v.s: Emperor Taizong of Liao led a large army south to attack Youzhou. The Later Tang generals Zhao Dejun and Zhao Yanshou defended the city, and the Khitan army besieged it for a long time without success. When Later Tang reinforcements arrived, the Liao army withdrew north, but not before carrying off a large number of captives and livestock.

v.t: Tsar Peter I of Bulgaria ascends the throne
v.s: Tsar Simeon I of Bulgaria died and was succeeded by his son Peter I. After his accession, Peter made peace with Byzantium, recognizing the Byzantine emperor as "father of the Bulgarian emperor" and marrying a Byzantine princess. Bulgaria turned toward peaceful development.

v.t: Duan Siping rebels in the former Nanzhao territory
v.s: Duan Siping, a general of Dayining, discontented with the tyranny of Yang Ganzhen, raised a rebellion in alliance with the thirty-seven tribes of eastern Yunnan. Duan's slogan was "lower taxes, punish corrupt officials," earning him support from all ethnic groups. Two years later he established the Dali Kingdom.

d.u: Emperor Mingzong of Later Tang (Li Siyuan)

v.t: King Ma Yin of Chu dies; Ma Xisheng succeeds
v.s: Ma Yin, the founding monarch of the Chu Kingdom during the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period, died in Changsha at the age of seventy-nine. His son Ma Xisheng succeeded him, continuing to use the title of King of Chu. Ma Xisheng was extravagant and wasteful, and the power of Chu began to decline.

v.t: Fatimids move capital to new city of Cairo
v.s: Fatimid Caliph al-Mu'izz moved his capital from Mahdiyya in Tunisia to new Cairo (modern Cairo) in Egypt. He built palaces, mosques, and walls along the east bank of the Nile, and Cairo thereafter became a political and cultural center of the Islamic world.

v.t: King Wang Geon of Goryeo issues the Ten Injunctions
v.s: Taejo Wang Geon of Goryeo issued the Ten Injunctions as a general guide for his successors. The tenets included upholding Buddhism, protecting local powerful families, being cautious in foreign wars, and selecting officials. This document became the foundation of the Goryeo dynasty.

v.t: Fujiwara no Tadahira compiles the Engi Kyaku in Japan
v.s: Fujiwara no Tadahira, on the order of Emperor Daigo, completed the Engi Kyaku (supplementary penal codes). This code upheld court authority and restricted the expansion of private estates, and it was the last major code of the Japanese ritsuryō period.

d.u: Emperor Mingzong of Later Tang (Li Siyuan)

v.t: Emperor Mingzong of Later Tang reforms finances, reduces taxes
v.s: Emperor Mingzong of Later Tang appointed Ren Huan as chancellor to reform state finances. He abolished various oppressive taxes imposed during Zhuangzong's reign, unifying land taxes into the "summer and autumn tax" of one dou of grain per mu. He also severely punished corrupt officials, allowing the local economy to recover.

v.t: Constitutional status of the Icelandic Althing is established
v.s: The chieftains of Iceland jointly adopted the Constitution of the Icelandic Free State, making the Althing the supreme legislative and judicial body, meeting each summer. The constitution also created the position of Law-Speaker, who was responsible for reciting the law codes.

v.t: Henry I of East Francia suppresses the Bavarian rebellion
v.s: Arnulf, Duke of Bavaria, proclaimed himself king and attempted to break away. Henry I laid siege to Regensburg, and Arnulf surrendered, recognizing Henry's suzerainty. Bavaria was reincorporated into East Francia.

v.t: Pagan Kingdom of Burma begins building a pagoda
v.s: King Tannet of Pagan began constructing the first large pagoda, the Bu Paya Pagoda, on the banks of the Irrawaddy River. Fifty meters high, it marked the beginning of Buddhist architecture in Pagan. Over the next two hundred years, more than ten thousand pagodas would be built on the Pagan plain.

d.u: Emperor Mingzong of Later Tang (Li Siyuan)

v.t: Meng Zhixiang sets up a separatist regime in Shu
v.s: Emperor Mingzong of Later Tang appointed Meng Zhixiang as jiedushi of Xichuan. After arriving in Chengdu, Meng consolidated his army, excluded court influence, and gradually formed an independent kingdom. Eleven years later he would proclaim himself emperor in Chengdu, establishing the Later Shu.

v.t: Abd al-Rahman III of Córdoba conquers Ceuta
v.s: Caliph Abd al-Rahman III of Córdoba sent a fleet across the Strait of Gibraltar to capture the North African port of Ceuta. The Umayyads expanded into North Africa, controlling a strategic choke point in the western Mediterranean trade.

v.t: The Wakan Roeishū is compiled in Japan
v.s: The Japanese scholar Fujiwara no Kintō compiled the Wakan Roeishū, a collection of famous lines from Chinese poets and Japanese waka poets. The book reflects the Heian aristocracy's aesthetic taste for both Chinese literature and native poetry.

v.t: Magyars plunder Lombardy
v.s: Magyar horsemen crossed the Alps and invaded Lombardy in northern Italy. They sacked Pavia, Milan, and other cities, burned churches, and captured thousands of residents. King Hugh of Italy paid a ransom, and the Magyars withdrew.

d.u: Emperor Mingzong of Later Tang (Li Siyuan)

v.t: Later Tang begins block printing of the Nine Classics
v.s: Feng Dao, chancellor of Later Tang, petitioned Emperor Mingzong to approve the block printing of the Confucian Nine Classics by the Directorate of Education. This was the first large-scale official block printing in Chinese history. It took twenty years to complete and promoted the dissemination of the Confucian classics.

v.t: Caliphate of Córdoba at its zenith
v.s: Caliph Abd al-Rahman III of Córdoba built a lavish palace at Madinat al-Zahra in southern Spain; the population of Córdoba reached half a million, surpassing Constantinople. Its library held 400,000 volumes, making it the largest cultural center in Europe at the time.

v.t: King Ma Xifan of Chu expands Changsha
v.s: Ma Xifan, king of Chu, expanded the city walls of Changsha, building the Huichun Garden and the Tiancefu Palace. He modeled his court on the Tang system, establishing the Eighteen Scholars of the Tiancefu to attract literati. Changsha became one of the important cultural centers of the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period.

v.t: Al-Azhar Mosque in Egypt establishes a university
v.s: The Fatimid dynasty established a teaching institution at the Al-Azhar Mosque in Cairo, offering instruction in jurisprudence, grammar, and astronomy. It is considered one of the world's earliest universities, attracting Muslim scholars from far and wide.

d.u: Emperor Mingzong of Later Tang (Li Siyuan)

v.t: Emperor Mingzong of Later Tang dies
v.s: Emperor Mingzong of Later Tang, Li Siyuan, died of illness in Luoyang at the age of sixty-seven. During his eight-year reign he had worked hard and reduced the people's burdens, but in his later years he became suspicious and executed several high officials, leading to a struggle among his sons. His son Li Conghou succeeded him, known as Emperor Min.

v.t: Kievan Rus' and Byzantium sign a trade treaty
v.s: Grand Prince Igor of Kiev and Byzantine Emperor Romanos I concluded a trade treaty. It stipulated that Rus' merchants might reside in Constantinople for six months and trade duty-free, but they had to register their names and could not carry weapons. This was an important step in normalizing Rus'-Byzantine relations.

v.t: Emperor Min of Later Tang and Prince of Lu Li Congke oppose each other
v.s: Emperor Min sought to reduce the power of the provincial governors by issuing orders to transfer them. Li Congke, Prince of Lu (an adopted son of Emperor Mingzong), rose in rebellion at Fengxiang, and the Later Tang plunged into civil war. The following year Li Congke marched on Luoyang and proclaimed himself emperor.

v.t: William I, Duke of Normandy, conquers Brittany
v.s: William I (Longsword), Duke of Normandy, marched into the eastern part of the Brittany peninsula, capturing Rennes and Nantes and forcing the Duke of Brittany to recognize Norman suzerainty. The Duchy of Normandy expanded its territory.

d.u: Emperor Modi of Later Tang (Li Congke)
Emperor Gaozu of Later Shu (Meng Zhixiang)

v.t: Li Congke usurps the throne, civil war in Later Tang
v.s: Li Congke, Prince of Lu, marched on Luoyang; Emperor Min fled to a provincial town and was killed. Li Congke ascended the throne as the last emperor of Later Tang. He became suspicious of his general Shi Jingtang, leading to a conflict that two years later would cause Shi to invite the Khitans to destroy Later Tang.

v.t: Meng Zhixiang establishes Later Shu
v.s: Taking advantage of the civil war in Later Tang, Meng Zhixiang proclaimed himself emperor in Chengdu with the dynastic name Shu (known historically as Later Shu). He continued to use the reign title of Changxing (of Emperor Mingzong of Later Tang) and sent forces east to capture Zhongzhou and Wanzhou, consolidating his rule over Shu.

v.t: Henry I of Germany defeats Danish Vikings
v.s: Henry I, King of East Francia, led his army north, defeating the Danish Vikings at Schleswig. He captured Hedeby, forced the Danish king to submit, and established a market town there.

v.t: Dali Kingdom is formally established
v.s: Duan Siping stormed Taihe City (present-day Dali), the capital of Dayining, and captured Yang Ganzhen. Duan Siping established the Dali Kingdom with its capital at Yangjumie. He introduced the Bazhao system and formed an alliance with the thirty-seven tribes, ushering in the Dali era in Yunnan.

d.u: Emperor Modi of Later Tang (Li Congke)

v.t: Civil war in Min: sons of Wang Shenzhi fight for the throne
v.s: After the death of Wang Shenzhi, the founding ruler of the Min Kingdom, his sons fought over the succession. Wang Yanhan, Wang Yanjun, Wang Yanxi, and Wang Yanzheng attacked each other in succession, plunging Min into a prolonged civil war. Southern Tang took advantage of this to destroy Min in 945.

v.t: King Wang Geon of Goryeo dies; Hyejong succeeds
v.s: Taejo Wang Geon of Goryeo died of illness, succeeded by his son Wang Mu, known as Hyejong. On his deathbed, Wang Geon urged his descendants to remain friendly with the Song dynasty and defend against the Khitans. Hyejong reigned for only two years before his death.

v.t: William I, Duke of Normandy, assassinated
v.s: William I, Duke of Normandy, was murdered by his own attendant; his son Richard I succeeded. King Louis IV of France invaded Normandy, but the young Richard I, with the support of his nobles, defeated the French army and consolidated his ducal position.

v.t: Persian poet Ferdowsi begins composing the Shahnameh
v.s: Ferdowsi, a poet of the Samanid court, began composing the Shahnameh, a Persian national epic chronicling Persian myths and the lives of its kings. This monumental work took over thirty years and became a cornerstone of Persian culture.

d.u: Emperor Gaozu of Later Jin (Shi Jingtang)

v.t: Shi Jingtang cedes the Sixteen Prefectures, destroys Later Tang, establishes Later Jin
v.s: Shi Jingtang, jiedushi of Hedong of Later Tang, rebelled in Taiyuan. To seek military aid, he declared himself a subject and a son to Emperor Taizong of Liao, Yelu Deguang, and ceded the Sixteen Prefectures of Yanyun (present-day Beijing, northern Hebei, northern Shanxi). The Khitans helped Shi Jingtang destroy Later Tang, and Shi proclaimed himself emperor, establishing the Later Jin dynasty. The loss of the Sixteen Prefectures deprived the Central Plains of its northern defensive barrier, affecting Song–Liao relations for three hundred years.

v.t: Goryeo establishes its capital at Kaegyeong
v.s: King Taejo Wang Geon built palaces and city walls at Kaegyeong (Kaesong) and formally made it his capital. He modeled the central government on the Tang system, establishing the Three Departments and Six Ministries and promulgating official rank grades. The basic structure of Goryeo's centralized government was thus established.

v.t: Otto I is crowned King of East Francia
v.s: Otto I, son of Henry I, was crowned King of East Francia at Aachen Cathedral. Ambitious, he was determined to strengthen royal power, suppress rebellious nobles, and began to meddle in Italian politics. He is considered the effective founder of the Holy Roman Empire.

v.t: Fatimid Caliph al-Mu'izz conquers Palestine
v.s: Fatimid Caliph al-Mu'izz sent his general Jawhar al-Rumi with an army to conquer Palestine, capturing Jerusalem, Ramla, and other cities. The Fatimids expanded into Asia, confronting the Abbasid Caliphate.

d.u: Emperor Gaozu of Later Jin (Shi Jingtang)

v.t: Southern Tang established, replacing Wu
v.s: Xu Zhigao (Li Bian), the powerful minister of Wu, usurped the throne and proclaimed himself emperor with the dynastic name Tang (known historically as Southern Tang), making Jinling (present-day Nanjing) his capital. Li Bian claimed descent from the Tang imperial house, reduced taxes and forced labor, built water conservancy works, and made Southern Tang the most prosperous regime in the south.

v.t: Dali Kingdom established
v.s: Duan Siping formally established the Dali Kingdom in Yunnan, with the reign name Wende. He made Buddhism the state religion, appointed Gao Fang as his chancellor, and implemented the "Four Garrisons" appanage system. The Dali Kingdom maintained long-term friendly relations with the Song and ruled Yunnan for more than three hundred years.

v.t: German King Otto I suppresses a rebellion
v.s: Otto I of East Francia crushed a joint rebellion by Eberhard, Duke of Bavaria, and Conrad, Duke of Franconia. He confiscated the rebels' lands and granted them to his own followers, strengthening royal power.

v.t: The Tengi Ritual is compiled in Japan
v.s: The Japanese noble Fujiwara no Morosuke compiled the Tengi Ritual, standardizing court ceremonies and rituals. It is an important source on ancient Japanese ritual systems.

d.u: Emperor Gaozu of Later Jin (Shi Jingtang)

v.t: Shi Jingtang honors Liao Taizong as his father emperor
v.s: Emperor Gaozu of Later Jin dispatched an envoy to Liao, presenting a memorial in which he declared himself a subject and addressed Liao Taizong Yelu Deguang as his "father emperor," referring to himself as the "son emperor." Liao Taizong in turn conferred on Shi Jingtang the title of "Emperor of Jin." This was a rare case of a Central Plains dynasty acknowledging a foreign state as its superior.

v.t: Otto I defeats Bohemia
v.s: Otto I of East Francia invaded the Duchy of Bohemia and defeated Duke Boleslaus I near Prague. Boleslaus was forced to submit and pay tribute, and Bohemia became a vassal of Germany.

v.t: Karakhanids capture Kashgar
v.s: Satuk Bughra Khan of the Karakhanid dynasty captured Kashgar at the western edge of the Tarim Basin and made it his eastern capital. Kashgar became an important center for the spread of Islamic culture into Xinjiang.

v.t: Buddhism is promoted in Goryeo
v.s: During the reign of King Taejo Wang Geon of Goryeo, Buddhism was declared the state religion. Wang Geon built Beopwangsa and Gwangsusa temples in Kaegyeong and invited eminent monks from China to lecture. Goryeo Buddhism afterwards flourished, especially the Seon (Zen) and Hwaeom schools.

d.u: Emperor Gaozu of Later Jin (Shi Jingtang)

v.t: Ngo Quyen proclaims himself king, Vietnam becomes independent
v.s: The Jiaozhi warlord Ngo Quyen, after defeating the Southern Han fleet at the Bach Dang River by using iron-tipped stakes and the tide, proclaimed himself king and built his capital at Co Loa (present-day Dong Anh District, Hanoi). He is known as King Ngo Quyen. Vietnam thus broke away from control by any central Chinese government, initiating its era of independence.

v.t: Byzantine navy recovers Crete
v.s: Emperor Romanos I of Byzantium sent a fleet to Crete; after an eight-month siege, the island was recovered from the Arab pirates who had held it for over a century. The Arabs on the island were forced to convert to Christianity, and Byzantium regained control of the eastern Mediterranean.

v.t: Emperor Suzaku of Japan ascends the throne
v.s: In the second year of the Tengyō era (939 CE), Emperor Suzaku ascended the throne. He was the 61st emperor of Japan. During his reign (930–946 CE, reign era changed to Tengyō in 936), the rebellion of Taira no Masakado (939 CE) and the rebellion of Fujiwara no Sumitomo (Tengyō–Jōhei Rebellion) broke out, having a profound impact on Japanese history.

v.t: Magyars plunder Burgundy in France
v.s: Magyar horsemen advanced deep into central France, sacking Sens, Auxerre, and other cities. They carried off large quantities of gold and burned churches. King Louis IV of West Francia was powerless to resist and had to pay tribute.

d.u: Emperor Gaozu of Later Jin (Shi Jingtang)

v.t: Later Shu casts Guangzheng Tongbao coins
v.s: Emperor Meng Chang of Later Shu cast "Guangzheng Tongbao" copper coins, standardizing currency in Shu. The economy of Later Shu prospered, and it was called the "Land of Abundance." However, Meng Chang was extravagant, using copper for palace construction, which led to inflation.

v.t: Pala dynasty builds temples in Bengal
v.s: Mahipala I, king of the Pala dynasty in Bengal, built the Odantapuri temple near Nalanda, dedicated to Buddhist Vajrayana. The Pala dynasty was the last major Buddhist dynasty in the Indian subcontinent; its architectural style influenced Buddhist art in Southeast Asia.

v.t: Grand Prince Igor of Kiev killed
v.s: Grand Prince Igor of Kiev was killed by the Drevlians when he demanded excessive tribute. His wife Olga became regent, took cruel revenge, and reformed the tribute system, establishing fixed amounts and collection points.

v.t: Li Bian of Southern Tang reforms tax laws
v.s: Li Bian, Emperor of Southern Tang, implemented a tax reform based on field measurement, abolishing various surtaxes from the Wu period and establishing a "seed loan" to help farmers buy seeds. Southern Tang agriculture thus recovered and grew.

v.t: Kievan Rus' attacks Constantinople
v.s: The new Grand Prince of Kiev, Sviatoslav I, led his army down the Dnieper River, crossed the Black Sea, and reached the Bosporus. Byzantine Emperor Constantine VII repelled the Rus' fleet with Greek fire and paid tribute to sue for peace.

v.t: Emperor Gaozu of Later Jin dies
v.s: Shi Jingtang died of illness in Luoyang at the age of fifty-one. His nephew Shi Chonggui succeeded him, known as Emperor Chu. The new emperor refused to continue calling himself a subject of the Khitans, leading to a breakdown in relations and eventually a Liao campaign that would destroy Later Jin.

v.t: Chinese calligrapher Yang Ningshi creates the Leek Flower Letter
v.s: The Five Dynasties calligrapher Yang Ningshi created the Leek Flower Letter (Jiuhua Tie) in Luoyang, a semi-cursive script work of elegant and free brushwork. It became an important model for later students of semi-cursive script.

v.t: Normans settle in southern Italy
v.s: The Norman mercenary leader Rainulf established a base at Aversa in southern Italy, the first permanent Norman settlement in the region. From there the Normans would gradually conquer all of southern Italy and establish the Kingdom of Sicily.

d.u: Emperor Gaozu of Later Jin (Shi Jingtang)

v.t: Shi Jingtang dies; Shi Chonggui succeeds
v.s: Emperor Gaozu of Later Jin died; his adopted son Shi Chonggui ascended the throne (Emperor Chu). The new emperor refused to call himself a subject of the Khitans, styling himself only as a "grandson emperor without claiming subject status," which enraged Liao Taizong Yelu Deguang, making war imminent.

v.t: Arab Translation Movement ends
v.s: The translation movement of the Abbasid Caliphate came to an end after about two centuries. The works of Aristotle, Plato, Euclid, Galen, and other Greek masters had mostly been translated into Arabic. Scholars in Baghdad began to produce original works on these foundations.

v.t: Chai Rong, later Emperor Shizong of the Song dynasty, is born
v.s: Chai Rong was born in Xingzhou (present-day Xingtai, Hebei). He later became the adopted son of his uncle Guo Wei and reigned as Emperor Shizong of Later Zhou, carrying out reforms that laid the groundwork for the reunification of China under the Song.

v.t: Olga of Kiev converts to Christianity
v.s: Olga, regent of Kiev, traveled to Constantinople and was baptized in the presence of Byzantine Emperor Constantine VII, taking the name Helena. She was the first Christian ruler in Rus', though her son Sviatoslav refused to convert.

d.u: Emperor Chu of Later Jin (Shi Chonggui)

v.t: Later Jin's relations with Khitans break down; war breaks out
v.s: Emperor Chu of Later Jin, in diplomatic correspondence with the Khitans, styled himself "grandson emperor" instead of "subject emperor" and refused to cede northern territories. Enraged, Liao Taizong Yelu Deguang led a large army south to attack Jin. The first Jin-Liao war began.

v.t: Classical Maya civilization completely collapses
v.s: The classical Maya cities of the southern lowlands—Palenque, Tikal, Copán, and others—were abandoned, and the population declined sharply. A combination of drought, warfare, and social collapse ended the classical Maya civilization. Northern cities like Chichen Itza continued to exist.

v.t: Carolingian Louis IV is imprisoned
v.s: Louis IV of West Francia was captured and imprisoned by Richard I, Duke of Normandy, after being defeated while attacking Normandy. He was released only after paying a ransom and ceding territory, and Carolingian prestige was destroyed.

v.t: Li Bian of Southern Tang dies; Li Jing succeeds
v.s: In the seventh year of the Sheng reign (943 CE), Li Bian of Southern Tang died. Li Bian had established the Southern Tang state and implemented policies of rest and recovery that made it the richest of the Ten Kingdoms. His son Li Jing, after succeeding, changed the conservative policy and conquered Min and Chu in succession, but the continuous wars exhausted the state's resources.

d.u: Emperor Chu of Later Jin (Shi Chonggui)

v.t: Jin-Liao war continues; Khitans invade in force
v.s: Liao Taizong again led 80,000 cavalry south, with the vanguard approaching Beizhou (Qinghe, Hebei). Later Jin generals Gao Xingzhou and Fu Yanqing resisted, and the two sides fought a stalemate at Chanzhou (Puyang). The Jin army used crossbows and catapults to repel the Khitan cavalry.

v.t: Fujiwara no Saneyori becomes Chancellor (Kampaku) in Japan
v.s: The Japanese noble Fujiwara no Saneyori was appointed Kampaku (the highest official assisting the emperor), ushering in the heyday of the Fujiwara regency (Sekkan) system. The emperor became a ceremonial figure, with real power held by the Fujiwara.

v.t: Kingdom of Croatia repulses a Hungarian invasion
v.s: King Krešimir I of Croatia led his army to defeat a Magyar invasion on the Drava River, protecting the Dalmatian coast. At this time Croatia was the most powerful Slavic state in the Balkans.

v.t: Arab geographer al-Istakhri draws a world map
v.s: The Persian geographer al-Istakhri completed his Book of Roads and Kingdoms, accompanied by colored maps depicting all Islamic lands from Spain to the Indus Valley. This represented the highest achievement of Islamic geography in the 10th century.

d.u: Emperor Chu of Later Jin (Shi Chonggui)

v.t: Battle of Yangcheng: Later Jin crushes the Khitans
v.s: Liao Taizong Yelu Deguang led 80,000 cavalry to besiege Yangcheng (present-day Qingyuan, Hebei). The Jin generals Fu Yanqing and Huangfu Yu led their troops out of the city to engage, using several thousand armored warhorses to charge the Khitan line. The Khitan army was routed, and Yelu Deguang fled north on a camel-drawn cart. After this battle, the Khitans did not dare invade the south for several years.

v.t: Southern Tang destroys Min
v.s: Taking advantage of the civil war in Min, Emperor Yuanzong Li Jing of Southern Tang sent generals Zha Wenwei and Bian Hao with an army into Fujian. The Min king Wang Yanzheng surrendered, and the Min kingdom fell. Southern Tang occupied most of Fujian, though Wuyue seized the opportunity to take Fuzhou.

v.t: Fatimid caliph al-Mu'izz moves capital to Cairo
v.s: Fatimid Caliph al-Mu'izz formally moved his capital from Tunisia to Cairo, completing the shift of Fatimid power to Egypt. A massive building program began in Cairo, including expansion of the Al-Azhar Mosque and palace complexes, making Cairo a political and religious center of the Islamic world.

v.t: Welsh king Hywel Dda promulgates a law code
v.s: Hywel the Good, king of Wales, convened a council at Whitland to codify the first written law code of Wales. The code unified local customary laws, established the king's authority, and recognized certain rights for women and slaves.

d.u: Emperor Chu of Later Jin (Shi Chonggui)

v.t: Khitan destroy Later Jin
v.s: Taking advantage of the surrender of the Later Jin general Du Chongwei, Liao Taizong Yelu Deguang led his army directly to Kaifeng. The Later Jin emperor Chu, Shi Chonggui, was captured, and Later Jin fell. Yelu Deguang entered Kaifeng and proclaimed himself emperor under the dynasty name "Great Liao," changing the reign title to Datong, in an attempt to rule the Central Plains. However, his troops looted and killed, arousing popular anger, and he was forced to withdraw north after a few months.

v.t: Liu Zhiyuan proclaims himself emperor in Taiyuan, establishing Later Han
v.s: After the fall of Later Jin, Liu Zhiyuan, jiedushi of Hedong, proclaimed himself emperor in Taiyuan with the dynasty name Han (historically known as Later Han). He addressed Liao Taizong as his "uncle emperor," and changed his personal name to Liu Chang, claiming descent from the Han imperial clan. Later Han was the shortest-lived dynasty of the Five Dynasties, lasting only four years.

v.t: Sviatoslav I of Kiev comes of age
v.s: Sviatoslav, the posthumous son of Grand Prince Igor of Kiev, came of age and began his personal rule. He was a pure warrior prince, fighting all his life and never allowing baggage trains or cooking gear to slow his marches. He began a massive military expansion.

v.t: Abd al-Rahman III of Córdoba builds Madinat al-Zahra
v.s: To commemorate a favorite concubine, Caliph Abd al-Rahman III of Córdoba built Madinat al-Zahra in the outskirts of Córdoba. The palace had more than 4,000 marble columns and was adorned with gold and silver, a wonder of Islamic Spain.

d.u: Emperor Gaozu of Later Han (Liu Zhiyuan)

v.t: Liao Taizong withdraws north; Later Han occupies Kaifeng
v.s: Unable to hold the Central Plains, Liao Taizong Yelu Deguang led his army back north and died on the way at Luancheng (present-day Luancheng, Hebei). Emperor Gaozu of Later Han, Liu Zhiyuan, seized the opportunity to enter Kaifeng and made it his capital, changing the reign title to Qianyou. Later Han formally replaced Later Jin as the Central Plains dynasty.

v.t: Li Yichao asserts independence; Dingnan military command becomes autonomous
v.s: Li Yichao, chief of the Tangut tribe, took advantage of the chaos in Later Han to declare that the Dingnan military command would be hereditary and would not pay tribute to the court. This was a key step toward the independence of the Dingnan command, the predecessor of the Western Xia.

v.t: Otto I suppresses a rebellion in Swabia
v.s: Otto I of East Francia suppressed a rebellion by Duke Herman I of Swabia, confiscated his fiefs, and redistributed them. Otto gave most of the crown lands to his own relatives, consolidating the power base of the royal family.

v.t: The Teishin Kōki is compiled in Japan
v.s: The Japanese noble Fujiwara no Tadahira personally wrote the diary Teishin Kōki, which records court ceremonies, personnel changes, and social customs, making it an important historical source for the mid-Heian period.

d.u: Emperor Yin of Later Han (Liu Chengyou)

v.t: Liu Zhiyuan dies; Emperor Yin Liu Chengyou succeeds
v.s: Emperor Gaozu of Later Han, Liu Zhiyuan, died of illness at the age of thirty. His son Liu Chengyou succeeded him, known as Emperor Yin. At eighteen, the young emperor was suspicious and would soon clash with founding ministers Yang Bin, Shi Hongzhao, and others, leading to a coup.

v.t: Otto I defeats an invasion by West Francia
v.s: Otto I of East Francia met the invading army of King Louis IV of West Francia on the Rhine. Otto routed the French army and pursued them as far as Rheims. The two sides concluded a peace by which West Francia renounced its claims to Lorraine.

v.t: Southern Tang general Bian Hao destroys the Chu kingdom
v.s: Emperor Yuanzong Li Jing of Southern Tang sent his general Bian Hao with an army to storm Changsha, the capital of Chu. King Ma Xichong of Chu surrendered, and the Chu kingdom fell. Southern Tang temporarily controlled the middle Yangtze, but the military expenditure was so large that it soon lost its ability to rule effectively.

v.t: Bagrat III of Georgia unifies the country
v.s: Bagrat III inherited the throne of Abkhazia and later that of Kartli, unifying eastern and western Georgia. He established the Bagratid dynasty and made Kutaisi his capital. Georgia became an independent kingdom.

d.u: Emperor Yin of Later Han (Liu Chengyou)

v.t: Guo Wei guards Yedu, commands a large army
v.s: Emperor Yin of Later Han appointed Guo Wei as the military governor of Yedu and commander of Tianxiong Army, to defend against the Khitans. Guo Wei thus obtained control of the Later Han's most elite troops, laying the military foundation for his eventual replacement of the dynasty.

v.t: Great plague in Huainan, Southern Tang
v.s: In the seventh year of the Baoda reign (949 CE), a massive plague broke out in the Huainan region (present-day Anhui and northern Jiangsu) of Southern Tang. The epidemic killed a large number of people and severely damaged agricultural production. The Southern Tang court organized medical relief, but the efforts were limited. The plague weakened Southern Tang's power.

v.t: Kievan Rus' conquers Kazan
v.s: Grand Prince Sviatoslav led a campaign east, conquering the Volga Bulgars and Khazars and capturing Kazan. Kievan Rus' gained control of the Volga trade route, establishing contacts with the Muslim East.

v.t: Abbot Odo of Cluny becomes famous for his reforms
v.s: Abbot Odo of Cluny died. During his tenure he vigorously enforced the Rule of Saint Benedict, requiring monks to withdraw from the secular world and to devote themselves only to prayer and labor. The Abbey of Cluny became a center of religious reform in Europe.

d.u: Emperor Yin of Later Han (Liu Chengyou)

v.t: Emperor Yin of Later Han massacres ministers; Guo Wei rebels
v.s: Emperor Yin, suspicious of his ministers, staged a coup and killed Yang Bin, Shi Hongzhao, and others, and secretly ordered Guo Wei's death. Guo Wei raised his army in Yedu and marched south. Emperor Yin was defeated and killed outside Bianzhou. Guo Wei entered Kaifeng and invited the empress dowager to act as regent.

v.t: Earliest recorded formula for gunpowder
v.s: A Daoist alchemical text called "True Essential Methods of the Mysterious Way" (Zhenyuan Miaodao Yaolüe), dating from the late Tang or Five Dynasties period, described an explosive phenomenon: "If one burns sulfur, realgar, saltpeter and honey together, flames rise, burning the hands and face and even setting the house on fire." This is the world's earliest recorded formula for gunpowder.

v.t: Polynesians discover New Zealand
v.s: The Polynesian navigator Kupe, leading two canoes from the Society Islands, after a long voyage discovered the North Island of New Zealand. Returning to Rarotonga, he reported the existence of this new land.

v.t: Kilwa becomes an East African trade hub
v.s: The East African coastal city-state of Kilwa (present-day Tanzania) began minting its own copper coins and became a center of the gold and ivory trade. The rulers of Kilwa controlled the export of gold from the Sofala mines (in modern Mozambique), making them immensely wealthy.

d.u: Emperor Taizu of Later Zhou (Guo Wei)

v.t: Guo Wei establishes Later Zhou
v.s: Guo Wei proclaimed himself emperor in Kaifeng with the dynasty name Zhou (known historically as Later Zhou), changing the reign title to Guangshun. Of humble origins, Guo Wei lived frugally, abolished the harsh taxes of Later Han, and released a large number of palace women. He was one of the most outstanding monarchs of the Five Dynasties.

v.t: Liu Min establishes Northern Han
v.s: Liu Min, younger brother of Liu Zhiyuan (Gaozu of Later Han), proclaimed himself emperor in Taiyuan, keeping the dynastic name Han (historically known as Northern Han). He allied himself with the Khitans, calling Liao Muzong his "uncle emperor," and became the only one of the Ten Kingdoms to establish a separatist regime in the north.

v.t: Liao Shizong Yelü Ruan is killed; Muzong succeeds
