v.s: Liao Shizong Yelü Ruan was assassinated in Guihua Prefecture (present-day Xuanhua, Hebei) by the clansman Chage and others. Yelü Jing put down the rebellion and succeeded him, becoming Emperor Muzong of Liao. Muzong was a drunkard who ignored state affairs, and the Liao dynasty entered a period of decline.

v.t: Byzantines recover parts of Syria
v.s: Byzantine Emperor Constantine VII sent the general Leo Phokas to capture Aleppo, forcing the Hamdanids to pay tribute. The Byzantines recovered some lost territory in northern Syria, but Antioch remained in Arab hands.

d.u: Emperor Taizu of Later Zhou (Guo Wei)

v.t: Later Zhou suppresses the rebellion of Murong Yanchao
v.s: Emperor Taizu of Later Zhou personally led a campaign to Yanzhou to suppress the rebellion of the military governor Murong Yanchao, who set himself on fire and died. Guo Wei ordered a reduction in taxes and a stabilization of local order.

v.t: Otto I campaigns in Italy
v.s: At the request of Pope John XII, Otto I of East Francia crossed the Alps into Italy to suppress a rebellion of Roman nobles. Otto was crowned King of Italy at Pavia.

v.t: Shwezigon Pagoda built in Pagan, Burma
v.s: In 952 CE, the Shwezigon Pagoda was built in the capital of the Pagan Kingdom in Myanmar. The pagoda was commissioned by King Anawrahta to enshrine a Buddha relic. The Shwezigon is a typical Burmese bell-shaped stupa.

v.t: Japanese poetry anthology Mura no Miyuki compiled
v.s: Emperor Murakami of Japan ordered the compilation of the waka anthology Mura no Miyuki, which collected excellent works by court poets. This period is considered the peak of Japanese waka poetry.

v.t: Otto I is crowned King of Germany
v.s: Otto I was crowned for a second time at Aachen Cathedral, this time explicitly as "King of Germany." In his coronation oath he referred to his kingdom as the "Roman Empire" for the first time.

v.t: Byzantine Emperor Constantine VII completes De Administrando Imperio
v.s: Constantine VII of Byzantium completed De Administrando Imperio, a detailed account of the Byzantine Empire's diplomatic relations and administrative systems with neighboring peoples. It is an important source on 10th-century international relations.

v.t: Southern Tang emperor Li Jing moves the capital to Nanchang
v.s: To avoid the threat of Later Zhou, Emperor Li Jing of Southern Tang moved his capital from Jinling to Hongzhou (present-day Nanchang), renaming it Nan Du (Southern Capital). However, due to opposition from his ministers, he soon moved it back to Jinling.

v.t: Karakhanids conquer Transoxiana
v.s: The Karakhanids captured Bukhara and Samarkand, the capitals of the Samanids, completely swallowing up Transoxiana. The last Samanid king fled into exile. The Karakhanids became the dominant power in Central Asia.

d.u: Emperor Shizong of Later Zhou (Chai Rong)

v.t: Guo Wei dies; Chai Rong succeeds
v.s: Emperor Taizu of Later Zhou, Guo Wei, died of illness and was succeeded by his adopted son Chai Rong, known as Emperor Shizong of Later Zhou. Chai Rong, the most outstanding monarch of the Five Dynasties, vowed to "spend ten years opening up the realm, ten years nurturing the people, and ten years bringing about great peace." He immediately launched a series of reforms.

v.t: Battle of Gaoping: Emperor Shizong of Later Zhou crushes the Northern Han–Liao coalition
v.s: Liu Min of Northern Han invaded the south with Khitan cavalry. Emperor Shizong of Later Zhou led his army in person to meet them. The two armies fought fiercely at Gaoping (present-day Jincheng, Shanxi). The right flank of the Zhou army collapsed, but Chai Rong led his own elite cavalry in a charge, killing the Northern Han general Zhang Yuanhui. Liu Min fled in panic, and the Khitans withdrew. This battle secured Chai Rong's position.

v.t: Otto I crushes a rebellion in Swabia
v.s: Otto I's son Ludolf, in alliance with Swabian nobles, rebelled. Otto laid siege to the rebel castle; Ludolf eventually surrendered and was pardoned but lost his title and lands.

v.t: Caliphate of Córdoba controls Morocco
v.s: The army of Caliph Abd al-Rahman III of Córdoba crossed the Strait of Gibraltar and captured Fez in Morocco. The Umayyads came to control the Maghreb region of North Africa, confronting the Fatimids.

d.u: Emperor Shizong of Later Zhou (Chai Rong)

v.t: Emperor Shizong of Later Zhou suppresses Buddhism
v.s: To increase state revenues, Emperor Shizong of Later Zhou ordered the suppression of unlicensed monasteries and forced monks and nuns to return to lay life. Nationwide, more than 33,000 temples were abolished, more than 60,000 monks and nuns were laicized, and bronze statues were melted down to cast coinage. This was the third large-scale suppression of Buddhism in Chinese history.

v.t: Battle of Lechfeld: Otto I annihilates the Magyar cavalry
v.s: Otto I of East Francia met the Magyar cavalry in a decisive battle on the Lechfeld near Augsburg. Otto used heavy cavalry and infantry squares to counter the Magyar light horse, annihilating the invaders and killing the Magyar chieftain. This battle ended more than a century of Magyar raids, forcing them to settle in Hungary and accept Christianity.

v.t: Emperor Shizong of Later Zhou campaigns against Southern Tang
v.s: Chai Rong personally led a southern campaign against Southern Tang, capturing Chuzhou, Yangzhou, and other cities. Li Jing of Southern Tang sued for peace, ceding the fourteen prefectures north of the Yangtze and acknowledging vassalage. The Later Zhou gained the salt and iron wealth of Huainan, greatly increasing its power.

v.t: Islamic scholar al-Ghazali is born
v.s: The Persian Islamic philosopher and jurist al-Ghazali was born in Tus (near present-day Mashhad, Iran). He later became one of the greatest theologians of Islam, authoring classics such as The Revival of the Religious Sciences.

d.u: Emperor Shizong of Later Zhou (Chai Rong)

v.t: Later Zhou again campaigns against Southern Tang
v.s: Emperor Shizong of Later Zhou launched a second southern campaign, capturing Shouzhou (present-day Shou County, Anhui) from Southern Tang and taking the Southern Tang general Liu Renzhan prisoner. Southern Tang completely lost its Huai River defenses and sent envoys to sue for peace.

v.t: Holy Roman Emperor Otto I is crowned emperor
v.s: Otto I, at the invitation of Pope John XII, once more entered Italy and was crowned "Roman Emperor" at St. Peter's Basilica in Rome. This is generally regarded as the beginning of the Holy Roman Empire. Otto I promised to protect the pope and the Roman Church.

v.t: Fujiwara regency promotes the Engi Shiki in Japan
v.s: Chancellor Fujiwara no Saneyori forced the implementation of the provisions of the Engi Shiki, cracking down on illegal private estates of local strongmen and increasing government revenue. However, local resistance was strong, and the reforms had limited effect.

v.t: Walls of Damascus are rebuilt
v.s: The Hamdanids rebuilt the walls and citadel of Damascus, which had been damaged by an earthquake, to defend against Byzantine attacks. Damascus once again became a military stronghold in northern Syria.

d.u: Emperor Shizong of Later Zhou (Chai Rong)

v.t: Emperor Shizong of Later Zhou's third campaign against Southern Tang
v.s: Chai Rong launched a third southern campaign, capturing Sizhou and Haozhou. Li Jing of Southern Tang sent envoys to present a memorial, abandoning his imperial title and acknowledging Later Zhou as his superior. Southern Tang surrendered the fourteen prefectures north of the Yangtze, and Emperor Shizong accepted the peace.

v.t: Byzantine general Nikephoros Phokas recovers Crete
v.s: The Byzantine general Nikephoros Phokas led a fleet to capture Chandax (modern Heraklion), the capital of the Cretan emirate, completely clearing the island of Arab pirates. Byzantium regained control of the eastern Mediterranean.

v.t: Liu Min of Northern Han dies; Liu Chengjun succeeds
v.s: In 957 CE, Liu Min (Shizu of Northern Han) died of illness, and his son Liu Chengjun succeeded. Liu Chengjun continued to rely on the Khitans (Liao dynasty) for support. Northern Han was the last of the Ten Kingdoms to fall (conquered by the Northern Song in 979).

v.t: Mieszko I of Poland establishes the Piast dynasty
v.s: Mieszko I, Duke of Poland, united the Polish tribes along the Warta River and established the Piast dynasty. He allied with the Holy Roman Empire and married the Czech princess Dobrawa.

d.u: Emperor Shizong of Later Zhou (Chai Rong)

v.t: Southern Tang acknowledges vassalage to Later Zhou
v.s: Li Jing of Southern Tang formally sent envoys to acknowledge vassalage to Later Zhou, renouncing his imperial title and calling himself "Lord of the Jiangnan State." Later Zhou invested him as "Lord of Tang." Southern Tang thereafter no longer used an imperial title and became a vassal of Later Zhou.

v.t: Great Mosque of Córdoba is expanded
v.s: Caliph Hakam II of Córdoba ordered an expansion of the Great Mosque of Córdoba, adding eight new rows of columns and constructing a richly decorated mihrab (prayer niche). The Great Mosque of Córdoba became one of the largest mosques in the Islamic world.

v.t: Sviatoslav of Kiev campaigns against the Khazars
v.s: Grand Prince Sviatoslav of Kiev led a large army to capture Sarkel (on the Don River), the capital of the Khazar Khaganate. The Khazar Khaganate thereafter declined, and Kievan Rus' gained control of the Volga and Don trade routes.

v.t: Japan compiles the Hyakka Myōshū
v.s: The Japanese scholar Minamoto no Shitagō compiled the twenty-volume Hyakka Myōshū, a classified Sino-Japanese dictionary containing definitions of words in astronomy, geography, history, official posts, and other fields.

d.u: Emperor Shizong of Later Zhou (Chai Rong)

v.t: Chai Rong campaigns north against the Khitans, recovers Guannan
v.s: Emperor Shizong of Later Zhou personally led a campaign against the Khitans, capturing Ningzhou, Mozhou, Yingzhou, Yizhou, and other prefectures. He recovered three prefectures and seven counties of the Sixteen Prefectures of Yanyun. Just as he was about to attack Youzhou, Chai Rong suddenly fell gravely ill and withdrew his army south.

v.t: Chai Rong dies; young Chai Zongxun succeeds
v.s: Emperor Shizong of Later Zhou died of illness in Kaifeng, only thirty-eight years old. His son Chai Zongxun succeeded him at the age of seven. Zhao Kuangyin was then the commander of the palace guard and controlled the imperial army. The following year, Zhao launched the Chenqiao mutiny and established the Song dynasty.

v.t: Byzantine Emperor Nikephoros II Phokas ascends the throne
v.s: The celebrated Byzantine general Nikephoros II Phokas, acclaimed by the army for his military achievements, forced Empress Theophano to abdicate. After his accession, he continued the war against the Arabs, recovering Crete and Cyprus.

v.t: Mieszko I of Poland accepts Christianity
v.s: To avoid invasion by the Holy Roman Empire, Mieszko I, Duke of Poland, accepted Latin Christianity and married the Czech princess Dobrawa. Poland thus joined the Christian world.

v.t: Chenqiao Mutiny: Zhao Kuangyin establishes the Song dynasty
v.s: Zhao Kuangyin, the commander of the palace guard of Later Zhou, was proclaimed emperor by his troops at Chenqiao (present-day Fengqiu, Henan) in a mutiny. He returned to Kaifeng and forced the young Chai Zongxun to abdicate, founding the Song dynasty with its capital at Kaifeng, historically known as the Northern Song. Zhao Kuangyin, Emperor Taizu of Song, subsequently "released military power over a cup of wine" (beijiu shi bingquan), stripping his generals of command and substituting civilian control, thereby ending the phenomenon of military domination that had characterized the Five Dynasties.

v.t: Ghaznavid dynasty founded
v.s: Alp Tigin, a Turkic slave commander, founded the Ghaznavid dynasty at Ghazni in Afghanistan, still nominally loyal to the Abbasid caliph. This dynasty, under Mahmud of Ghazni, would later expand into the Indus Valley.

v.t: Otto I is crowned Holy Roman Emperor
v.s: Otto I had already been crowned in Rome several times. In 960 he suppressed a rebellion of the Roman nobles, confirming his role as protector of the pope. The Holy Roman Empire formally established its suzerainty over north-central Italy.

v.t: Emperor Taizu of Song reforms the central administration
v.s: Zhao Kuangyin abolished the old Later Zhou system, establishing the "Zhongshu Menxia" as the highest administrative organ, and simultaneously created the Privy Council (shumiyuan) to control military affairs and the Three Departments (sansi) to control finance, dividing the power of the chancellor. The system of the Two Palaces and Three Departments (erfu sansi) became the foundation of Song officialdom.

v.t: Releasing military power over a cup of wine
v.s: Emperor Taizu of Song hosted a banquet for his senior military commanders, including Shi Shouxin and Gao Huaide. He lamented that being emperor was less secure than being a military governor, implying that they should resign their commands. The next day, all the generals pleaded illness and asked to be discharged. Zhao Kuangyin rewarded them with gold, silver, and estates, and appointed civilian officials to command the imperial army. This completely solved the problem of military mutiny that had plagued the Five Dynasties.

v.t: Gwangjong of Goryeo reforms the civil service examination
v.s: King Gwangjong of Goryeo reformed the civil service examination, establishing the jinshi (advanced scholar) and mingjing (classics) exams modeled on the Tang dynasty. He also implemented a law to return slaves held illegally by aristocrats to the state.

v.t: King Harald Bluetooth of Denmark converts to Christianity
v.s: King Harald Bluetooth of Denmark, persuaded by German missionaries, accepted baptism and ordered the construction of a wooden church at Jelling. Denmark became a Christian kingdom.

v.t: Byzantines capture Aleppo
v.s: Byzantine Emperor Nikephoros II Phokas personally led an eastern campaign, capturing the northern Syrian stronghold of Aleppo. The Hamdanids were forced to acknowledge vassalage. Byzantine power returned to the Middle East.

v.t: Otto I is crowned Holy Roman Emperor [962]
v.s: Otto I was crowned "Roman Emperor" by Pope John XII in Rome. This marks the formal establishment of the Holy Roman Empire. Otto's coronation ceremony included a promise to protect the pope, but also established the emperor's suzerainty over the papacy.

v.t: Emperor Taizu of Song establishes the Three Departments
v.s: Zhao Kuangyin established the Three Departments (Salt and Iron, Expenditure, and Census) to control finances, headed by a Commissioner of the Three Departments (sansi shi) whose rank was just below the chancellor. Financial power was thus separated from the chancellor's office, strengthening the emperor's control over the economy.

v.t: Sviatoslav of Kiev campaigns against Bulgaria
v.s: Grand Prince Sviatoslav of Kiev invaded the First Bulgarian Empire, capturing Pereyaslavets on the lower Danube, with the intention of moving his capital there. The Byzantines felt threatened, and relations with the Rus' became strained.

v.t: The Kagerō Nikki is written in Japan
v.s: The Japanese noblewoman Fujiwara no Michitsuna no Haha began writing the autobiographical Kagerō Nikki (Gossamer Years), telling of her unhappy marriage. It is a classic of Japanese women's literature.

v.t: Northern Song destroys Jingnan and Hunan
v.s: Emperor Taizu of Song used the pretext of aiding Hunan to send Murong Yanzhao and Li Chuyun south. Gao Jichong of Jingnan surrendered. The Song army then advanced into Hunan, capturing Zhou Baoquan. Jingnan and Hunan were incorporated into the Northern Song.

v.t: Byzantines recover Cyprus
v.s: Byzantine Emperor Nikephoros II Phokas sent a fleet to capture Cyprus, taking the strategic eastern Mediterranean island from the Arabs. Byzantium gained control of eastern Mediterranean sea lanes.

v.t: Harald Fairhair of Norway dies
v.s: Harald Fairhair, the first king to unify Norway, died. He had unified the petty kingdoms of Norway, but after his death the kingdom broke apart. His grandson Olaf I would later reunify it.

v.t: Karakhanids conquer Khwarazm
v.s: Ali Tegin of the Karakhanids led his army to conquer Khwarazm (south of the Aral Sea), spreading Islam to the northern part of Central Asia. Khwarazm thereafter became a center of Islamic culture.

v.t: Northern Song attacks Later Shu
v.s: Emperor Taizu of Song sent three armies under Wang Quanbin, Cui Yanjin, and Liu Guangyi to attack Later Shu. The northern force advanced into Shu from Fengzhou, while the eastern force went up the Yangtze from Guizhou. Meng Chang, the ruler of Later Shu, was weak and incompetent, and his main forces collapsed at the first blow.

v.t: Christianization of Silesia
v.s: Holy Roman Emperor Otto I established a bishopric in Silesia on the border with Poland, forcing the local Slavic peoples to convert to Christianity. This was the first step in German eastward expansion.

v.t: Goryeo mints and circulates Cheonwon Jungbo coins
v.s: King Gwangjong of Goryeo, imitating Tang dynasty coins, cast Cheonwon Jungbo iron coins and put them into circulation. This was the first large-scale minting of coins by the Goryeo dynasty, though the common people still preferred to trade with cloth.

v.t: Abbasid caliph al-Muti ascends the throne
v.s: In 964 CE, Abbasid caliph al-Muti ascended the throne in Baghdad. At this time, the Abbasid caliphs had become puppets of the Buwayhids. Al-Muti was in fact a puppet installed by the Buwayhid emir Bakhtiyar. The caliph's religious authority was still recognized, but his secular power had completely vanished.

v.t: Later Shu falls
v.s: The Song army entered Chengdu, and Meng Chang, the last ruler of Later Shu, surrendered. He was escorted to Kaifeng, created Duke of Qin, and died mysteriously a few days later. Song Taizu transported the treasures of Later Shu to Kaifeng, filling the state treasury.

v.t: Sviatoslav of Kiev conquers Bulgaria
v.s: Grand Prince Sviatoslav of Kiev decisively defeated Tsar Boris II of Bulgaria and occupied the Bulgarian capital, Preslav. Byzantine Emperor Nikephoros II Phokas came into conflict with the Rus', and war broke out.

v.t: Fujiwara no Kaneie becomes Chancellor (Kampaku) in Japan
v.s: In 965 CE (Kōhō 2), Fujiwara no Kaneie became Kampaku (regent). The Fujiwara clan wielded real power during the Heian period by marrying their daughters to emperors and acting as regents during the minority of the emperor. Fujiwara no Kaneie was the father of Fujiwara no Michinaga and paved the way for the Fujiwara's golden age.

v.t: King Bagrat III of Georgia dies
v.s: Bagrat III, the founder of a unified Georgia, died, and was succeeded by his son George I. Georgia began to grow stronger, but soon afterward went to war with Byzantium.

v.t: Emperor Taizu of Song perfects the civil service examination system
v.s: Emperor Taizu of Song formally established the triennial examination system and created the "palace examination," personally presiding over the final test. He also ordered the adoption of name-blanketing (糊名, huming) to prevent examiner fraud. The improvement of the examination system brought a large number of scholars from humble backgrounds into the bureaucracy.

v.t: Northern Song strengthens pressure on southern states
v.s: Having destroyed Jingnan, Hunan, and Later Shu, the Northern Song continued to exert military pressure on Southern Han and Southern Tang, inducing Wuyue and Min to submit. The various southern states sent tribute, and the trend toward unification under the Song became clear.

v.t: Mieszko I of Poland establishes an independent diocese
v.s: Mieszko I, Duke of Poland, requested the establishment of a bishopric at Poznań, directly under the pope, to free himself from German ecclesiastical control. This step strengthened the independence of the Polish church.

v.t: Fatimid caliph al-Mu'izz captures Damascus
v.s: In 966 CE, Fatimid caliph al-Mu'izz sent his general Jawhar al-Siqilli to conquer Damascus in Syria. The Fatimids had originated in North Africa, conquered Egypt in 969, and founded the city of Cairo. The conquest of Damascus marked the expansion of Fatimid power to the eastern Mediterranean coast.

v.t: Southern Han and Northern Song face off
v.s: In 967 CE, Southern Han (present-day Guangdong and Guangxi) and the Northern Song, which had already unified the Central Plains and most of southern China, came into direct confrontation. The emperor of Southern Han, Liu Chang, was dissolute and ignored government affairs. Emperor Taizu of Song, after pacifying Jinghu, prepared to march south. The fall of Southern Han was imminent.

v.t: Fatimids conquer Egypt
v.s: Fatimid caliph al-Mu'izz sent his general Jawhar al-Rumi with an army of 100,000 east to Egypt, capturing Fustat (south of modern Cairo) and destroying the Ikhshidid dynasty, the Abbasid vassal in Egypt. The Fatimids thereafter made Egypt their center.

v.t: Byzantine Emperor Nikephoros II Phokas is assassinated
v.s: Empress Theophano and her lover John Tzimiskes conspired to assassinate Emperor Nikephoros II in the palace. John succeeded him as emperor (John I).

v.t: Emperor En'yū of Japan ascends the throne
v.s: In 967 CE (Kōhō 4), Emperor En'yū ascended the throne. During his reign (967–969 CE), Fujiwara no Saneyori served as Kampaku, and the Fujiwara continued to control the court. En'yū's reign was short, and he abdicated to become a monk in 969.

v.t: Northern Song begins campaigns against Northern Han
v.s: Emperor Taizu of Song personally led a campaign against Northern Han, besieging Taiyuan. But Khitan reinforcements arrived, and the Song forces ran short of supplies, forcing a withdrawal. Northern Han continued to survive under Khitan protection.

v.t: Fatimids make Cairo their capital
v.s: Fatimid caliph al-Mu'izz ordered the construction of a new capital, Cairo (meaning "Victorious City"), north of Fustat, and moved his palace and government there. Cairo became the capital of the Fatimid Caliphate.

v.t: Sviatoslav of Kiev and Byzantium fight a decisive battle
v.s: Byzantine Emperor John I personally led his army to defeat the Rus' forces of Sviatoslav at Arcadiopolis on the Danube. The Rus' withdrew, and Sviatoslav was killed in battle the following year.

v.t: King Gwangjong of Goryeo dies
v.s: King Gwangjong of Goryeo died, and his son Wang Ju succeeded, known as King Gyeongjong. Gwangjong had implemented a civil service examination and the law checking slaves, but this caused discontent among the nobles. After his accession, Gyeongjong abolished some harsh laws to appease the powerful families.

v.t: Liao Muzong is killed; Jingzong succeeds
v.s: Liao Muzong Yelü Jing, because of his addiction to alcohol and his cruelty, was murdered by his attendants. Yelü Xian was enthroned as Emperor Jingzong. Jingzong promoted Han Chinese officials such as Han Derang, reduced taxes, and began to restore Liao's power.

v.t: Fatimids complete the conquest of Egypt
v.s: The Fatimid general Jawhar eliminated the last Abbasid remnants in Egypt and captured Alexandria. Egypt became the core region of the Fatimid Caliphate, providing a base for rivaling the Abbasids.

v.t: Byzantines recover Antioch
v.s: Byzantine Emperor John I led a siege of Antioch; after eight months, the Arab garrison surrendered. Byzantium recovered the seat of the oldest Christian patriarchate in the East.

v.t: The Sanjūrokkasen (Thirty-six Poets) anthology is compiled in Japan
v.s: In 969 CE (Anna 2), the anthology Sanjūrokkasen (Thirty-six Poets) was compiled. It gathered waka poems from thirty-six famous poets, ranging from Kakinomoto no Hitomaro to Ki no Tsurayuki. This anthology was one of the most important collections in the history of Japanese waka, profoundly influencing later poetry.

v.t: Northern Song destroys Southern Han
v.s: Emperor Taizu of Song sent Pan Mei south to attack Southern Han. The Song army captured Guangzhou, and Southern Han emperor Liu Chang surrendered. He was taken to Kaifeng, and Southern Han fell. The Northern Song gained sixty prefectures in Lingnan.

v.t: King Olaf I of Norway ascends the throne
v.s: In 970 CE (actually about 995), Olaf I Tryggvason became king of Norway. Olaf had lived in England and Russia and was baptized a Christian. He returned home, seized the throne, and forcefully imposed Christianity on Norway. Olaf was killed in the Battle of Svolder in 1000.

v.t: Mieszko I of Poland conquers Pomerania
v.s: Mieszko I, Duke of Poland, led his army to conquer Pomerania, on the south shore of the Baltic Sea, capturing the Oder River port of Szczecin. Poland gained access to the sea.

v.t: Islamic scholar al-Farabi dies
v.s: The Persian philosopher al-Farabi died in Baghdad. He was one of the most outstanding philosophers of the Islamic Golden Age, known as the "Second Teacher" (after Aristotle).

v.t: Northern Song consolidates Lingnan, implements pacification policies
v.s: After destroying Southern Han, Emperor Taizu of Song issued a three-year tax exemption, freed slaves, and sent civilian officials to govern Lingnan. The region stabilized, and the Song gained control of the gateway to South China Sea trade.

v.t: Christianization of Scandinavia accelerates
v.s: King Harald Bluetooth of Denmark had a runestone carved at Jelling, inscribed with his claim to have "made the Danes Christian." King Olaf I of Norway also used violence to promote Christianity. Christianity spread rapidly in Northern Europe.

v.t: King Gyeongjong of Goryeo establishes the Jeonsigwa (Land and Forest Classification System)
v.s: King Gyeongjong of Goryeo issued the Jeonsigwa system, distributing land and forests to officials according to rank, to stabilize the aristocratic economy. This system formed the basis of Goryeo land tenure and was used for centuries.

v.t: Al-Azhar University is founded at Al-Azhar Mosque in Cairo
v.s: The Fatimid dynasty formally established a university curriculum at the Al-Azhar Mosque, teaching theology, jurisprudence, grammar, and astronomy. It is considered one of the world's earliest universities.

v.t: Northern Song reorganizes the imperial guard; implements the rotating garrison system
v.s: Emperor Taizu of Song introduced the rotating garrison system (gengshu fa), requiring imperial guard units to change their garrison posts regularly, but their commanders did not move with them, achieving the principle that "the soldiers do not recognize their generals, and the generals do not command the soldiers." This prevented the emergence of warlords.

v.t: Otto II succeeds as Holy Roman Emperor
v.s: Otto I died, and his son Otto II succeeded. He married the Byzantine princess Theophanu, strengthening ties with Byzantium. He continued to suppress rebellious nobles in Germany.

v.t: Fujiwara no Michitaka becomes Chancellor (Kampaku) in Japan
v.s: In 972 CE (Tenroku 3), Fujiwara no Michitaka became Kampaku (chancellor). He was the son of Fujiwara no Kaneie and older brother of Fujiwara no Michinaga. During his tenure, the Fujiwara regency continued, but fierce power struggles within the family (Michitaka vs. Michinaga) continued.

v.t: Irish king Brian Boru defeats the Vikings
v.s: Brian Boru, king of Munster, defeated the Vikings at Limerick and unified southern Ireland. He styled himself "Emperor of the Irish," ending Viking domination in Ireland.

v.t: Zhao Kuangyin and Zhao Pu formulate policy on a snowy night
v.s: Emperor Taizu of Song visited his chancellor Zhao Pu on a snowy night to discuss a unification strategy. Zhao Pu proposed the "south first, then north" strategy: first pacify the southern kingdoms, then recover the Sixteen Prefectures of Yanyun. The emperor adopted this plan, accelerating unification.

v.t: Caliph Hisham II of Córdoba ascends the throne
v.s: Caliph Hakam II of Córdoba died, and his son Hisham II succeeded at the age of ten. The chief minister (hajib) al-Mansur seized actual power, becoming the de facto ruler of the Caliphate of Córdoba.

v.t: Đinh Bộ Lĩnh proclaims himself emperor, founding the Đinh dynasty in Vietnam
v.s: The Vietnamese local warlord Đinh Bộ Lĩnh, having pacified the Twelve Warlords, proclaimed himself Emperor Đại Thắng Minh, with the dynastic name Đại Cồ Việt, and made his capital at Hoa Lư (present-day Ninh Bình). This was the first independent dynasty in Vietnamese history, breaking away from China.

v.t: Grand Prince Sviatoslav of Kiev is killed in battle
v.s: On his way back to Kiev from Bulgaria, Grand Prince Sviatoslav was ambushed and killed by the Pechenegs. The Pecheneg chief made a drinking cup from his skull. His son Vladimir later succeeded him.

v.t: Northern Song attacks Southern Tang
v.s: Using as a pretext that Li Yu, the ruler of Southern Tang, had claimed to be ill and not appeared at court, Emperor Taizu of Song sent Cao Bin and Pan Mei with 100,000 troops south. The Song army built pontoon bridges to cross the Yangtze and advanced directly on Jinling. The Southern Tang army was routed.

v.t: Holy Roman Emperor Otto II suppresses the Bavarian rebellion
v.s: In 974 CE, Holy Roman Emperor Otto II suppressed a rebellion by Duke Henry II of Bavaria. Henry II (the Wrangler) rose in rebellion because he opposed Otto II's centralization policies. Otto II defeated the rebels and temporarily consolidated the empire's unity.

v.t: The Ochikubo Monogatari is written in Japan
v.s: Around 974 CE (Ten'en 2), the Ochikubo Monogatari (The Tale of the Fallen Ditch) was written. This is a notable work of Heian period literature, telling the story of a middle counselor's son named Ochikubo. It illustrates the romantic and social life of Heian aristocrats.

v.t: Byzantine Emperor John I dies
v.s: In 974 CE (actually 976 CE), Byzantine Emperor John I Tzimiskes died. He was an important emperor of the Byzantine Macedonian dynasty. During his reign (969–976 CE), he defeated the Bulgarians and the Rus', expanding the empire's territory in the Balkans and Syria.

v.t: Southern Tang falls; Li Yu is captured
v.s: Cao Bin stormed Jinling, and Li Yu, the last ruler of Southern Tang, surrendered. He was taken to Kaifeng and given the mocking title "Marquis Who Violated Orders." Southern Tang fell. Li Yu was a famous poet, and his ci lyrics remain immortal. The Northern Song had basically unified the south.

v.t: Grand Prince Vladimir of Kiev ascends the throne
v.s: Vladimir, son of Sviatoslav, regained the throne of Kiev. He initially worshiped the Norse gods, but in order to unify various groups he decided to adopt a monotheistic religion.

v.t: Fatimids build the walls of Cairo
v.s: In 975 CE, Fatimid caliph al-Mu'izz ordered the construction of walls around his newly built capital, Cairo. The Fatimids conquered Egypt in 969 and founded Cairo. The walls strengthened Cairo's defenses, making it a major city of the Middle East.

v.t: Emperor Taizu of Song reforms taxation and standardizes weights and measures
v.s: After unifying the south, Emperor Taizu ordered the nationwide standardization of weights and measures and introduced a system of leasing state lands to tenant farmers, distributing large amounts of waste land to landless peasants. The Song economy began to recover.

v.t: Emperor Taizu of Song dies; Emperor Taizong succeeds
v.s: Emperor Taizu of Song, Zhao Kuangyin, died suddenly in the Wansui Hall of Kaifeng at the age of fifty. His younger brother Zhao Guangyi succeeded him, becoming Emperor Taizong of Song. The event is known as the "candle and axe shadow" mystery. After his accession, Taizong changed the reign title to Taiping Xingguo.

v.t: Otto II invades Italy
v.s: Holy Roman Emperor Otto II led his army across the Alps to suppress a rebellion of northern Italian nobles, reestablishing imperial suzerainty over Italy.

v.t: Đinh Bộ Lĩnh sends envoys to the Song court
v.s: In 976 CE, after Đinh Bộ Lĩnh (Đinh Tiên Hoàng) had unified Vietnam, he sent envoys to the Northern Song to pay tribute and requested recognition from Emperor Taizu. Đinh Bộ Lĩnh was the first Vietnamese emperor to centralize power. The Song recognized Đinh's independence, investing him as King of Jiaozhi.

v.t: Islamic scholar al-Ghazali is born [976]
v.s: The Persian Islamic philosopher al-Ghazali was born in Tus. He later became the greatest theologian of Islam, criticizing the influence of Greek philosophy on Islamic thought.

v.t: Chen Hongjin submits to the Song
v.s: Chen Hongjin, who controlled Quanzhou (Qingyuan Military Command), surrendered the two prefectures of Zhang and Quanzhou to the Song. Emperor Taizong appointed him military commissioner of Wuning. Southern Fujian was completely incorporated into the Northern Song.

v.t: Otto II and Byzantium fight over southern Italy
v.s: Holy Roman Emperor Otto II sent an army to invade the Byzantine-controlled areas of southern Italy, capturing Taranto. The Byzantine army defeated Otto's forces in Calabria, and both sides agreed to a truce.

v.t: The Utsuho Monogatari is written in Japan
v.s: Around 977 CE (Jōgen 2), the Utsuho Monogatari (The Tale of the Hollow Tree) was begun. This work is one of the earliest "long monogatari" in Japan, focusing on the love lives of several aristocratic men and women in a court setting. It has a complex structure and is quite lengthy.

v.t: Karakhanids conquer Khotan
v.s: In 977 CE (actually about 1006 CE), the Karakhanid dynasty of Central Asia conquered the Buddhist kingdom of Khotan (modern Hotan, Xinjiang). The Karakhanids, under successors of Satuk Bughra Khan, launched a jihad against Khotan. The fall of Khotan marked the replacement of Buddhism by Islam as the ruling religion in the southwestern Tarim Basin.

v.t: Wuyue submits to the Song
v.s: King Qian Chu of Wuyue surrendered his thirteen prefectures to the Song. Qian Chu personally went to Kaifeng to pay court, and Emperor Taizong detained him there, granting him estates for his retirement. Wuyue fell, leaving only Northern Han among the Ten Kingdoms.

v.t: Song campaigns to destroy Northern Han begin
v.s: In the third year of the Taiping Xingguo era (978 CE), Emperor Taizong of Song began planning military operations to destroy Northern Han. Northern Han was the last of the Ten Kingdoms. The emperor personally led the campaign north, engaging the combined forces of Northern Han and the Liao near Taiyuan. Northern Han would fall in 979.

v.t: Harald Bluetooth of Denmark erects the Jelling Stones
v.s: King Harald Bluetooth of Denmark erected the Jelling Stones, a set of three runestones, one of which depicts Christ and bears an Old Norse inscription recording his unification of Denmark and his conversion to Christianity. The Jelling Stones have been called "Denmark's birth certificate."

v.t: Abbasid caliph at-Ta'i ascends the throne
v.s: In 978 CE, Abbasid caliph at-Ta'i ascended the throne in Baghdad. He too was a puppet of the Buwayhid dynasty. During at-Ta'i's reign (974–991 CE), civil war within the Buwayhid state intensified, rendering the caliph politically even more insignificant.

v.t: Northern Song destroys Northern Han, basically reunifies China
v.s: The Song army stormed Taiyuan, and the Northern Han emperor Liu Jiyuan surrendered. Northern Han fell. Emperor Taizong ordered the city of Taiyuan burned and razed to eliminate any future threat. Except for the Sixteen Prefectures of Yanyun, the core Chinese territory was reunified, ending the fragmentation of the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period.

v.t: Battle of Gaoliang River: Emperor Taizong of Song suffers a crushing defeat in his northern campaign
v.s: Taking advantage of the momentum of destroying Northern Han, Emperor Taizong of Song led his army north against the Khitans in an attempt to recover the Sixteen Prefectures of Yanyun. The Song army besieged Youzhou (modern Beijing). The Khitan general Yelü Xiuge led reinforcements to the rescue and decisively defeated the Song army on the Gaoliang River (northwest of present-day Beijing). The emperor was wounded by arrows and fled south on a donkey cart. The Song army suffered heavy losses, and the northern campaign failed.

v.t: King Olaf I of Norway is killed in battle
v.s: King Olaf I of Norway was defeated in a naval battle against a Danish–Swedish coalition and drowned himself. Norway was annexed by Denmark and did not regain independence until 1015.

v.t: Mahmud of Ghazni ascends the throne
v.s: Mahmud succeeded as Sultan of the Ghaznavid dynasty. He turned Ghazni into a powerful military empire in Central Asia, launching multiple invasions of India and plundering the treasures of the northern Indian princes.

v.t: Vladimir of Kiev is baptized
v.s: Grand Prince Vladimir of Kiev accepted baptism at Chersonesus (Korsun) in the Crimea, taking the name Basil. He then returned to Kiev and ordered the entire population to be baptized in the Dnieper River. The Rus' thus adopted Orthodox Christianity and entered the circle of Christian civilization.

v.t: Early formation of the Songhai Empire
v.s: The Songhai people along the Niger River in West Africa began to form a state. The Songhai ruler made Gao his capital, engaging in the gold and slave trade, gradually becoming a major power after the Ghana Empire.

v.t: Emperor Jingzong of Liao invades the south; fierce fighting on the Song-Liao border
v.s: Emperor Jingzong of Liao led his army south to attack Waqiao Pass (present-day Xiong County, Hebei). The Song generals Zhang Qixian and Tian Chongjin commanded the resistance, and the two sides fought a tug-of-war along the border. The Liao army looted and then withdrew.

v.t: King Æthelred II of England ascends the throne
v.s: Æthelred II the Unready became king of England. During his reign, Danish Vikings invaded in force, and England was forced to pay large sums of Danegeld to buy peace.

v.t: Northern Song builds fortifications at Dingzhou and Zhenzhou
v.s: To defend against the Khitans, the Song built city walls at Dingzhou, Zhenzhou, and other places, stockpiled provisions, and stationed heavy troops. The border generals Li Jilong and Tian Chongjin vigorously trained their troops.

v.t: Emperor Taizong of Song orders the compilation of the Taiping Yulan
v.s: Emperor Taizong commissioned Li Fang and others to compile the massive encyclopedia Taiping Yulan (Imperial Readings of the Taiping Era), in 1,000 volumes and 55 sections, drawing from classics, histories, philosophers, and literature. The work took six years and became a monumental Song-dynasty encyclopedia.

v.t: The historical work Ōkagami is compiled in Japan
v.s: Japanese nobles compiled the historical Ōkagami (Great Mirror), written in a question-and-answer style, relating events from Emperor Montoku to Emperor Go-Ichijō. This work is among the "four mirrors" of Japanese historiography and preserves Heian court records.

v.t: Byzantine Basil II suppresses a rebellion in Asia Minor
v.s: Byzantine Emperor Basil II (then twenty-three years old) led an army to suppress a rebellion of the Anatolian nobility. He confiscated the rebels' lands and distributed them to commoner soldiers, laying the foundation for a military power base.

v.t: Emperor Jingzong of Liao dies; Emperor Shengzong succeeds
v.s: Emperor Jingzong of Liao died of illness and was succeeded by his son Yelü Longxu, who was only twelve, becoming Emperor Shengzong. Empress Dowager Xiao Chuo acted as regent. She promoted the Han Chinese official Han Derang and carried out reforms, ushering in the peak period of the Liao dynasty.

v.t: Li Jiqian rebels against the Song; precursor of Western Xia rises
v.s: Li Jiqian, chief of the Tangut tribe, rebelled against the Song because the Song had taken back the territory of Xiazhou. He fled with his followers to Dijinze (present-day Inner Mongolia) and married a Khitan princess, receiving Liao recognition. The independence of Western Xia began at this time.

v.t: Erik the Red discovers Greenland
v.s: The Norwegian explorer Erik Thorvaldsson (Erik the Red), exiled from Iceland for murder, sailed west and discovered a large ice-covered land, which he named Greenland ("green land"). Three years later he led settlers there to establish a colony.

v.t: Holy Roman Emperor Otto II dies
v.s: In 982 CE (actually 983 CE), Holy Roman Emperor Otto II died in Rome at the age of 28. He had encountered defeat by Arab forces while campaigning in Italy and retreated, later dying in Rome. His son Otto III, only three years old, succeeded him, and the empire fell into turmoil.

v.t: Liao changes reign title to Tonghe; Empress Dowager Xiao Chuo acts as regent
v.s: Emperor Shengzong of Liao changed the reign title to Tonghe, and Empress Dowager Xiao Chuo formally assumed regency. She ordered tax reductions, streamlined administration, and personally led armies south against the Song, beginning a new phase of the Song-Liao war.

v.t: Fatimids build desert palaces
v.s: The Fatimid dynasty built palaces and gardens in desert oases near Cairo as retreats for the caliph. These buildings blended Fatimid and Persian styles and had great artistic value.

v.t: King Seongjong of Goryeo promotes Confucian rule
v.s: King Seongjong of Goryeo (r. 981–997) formally promoted a Confucian governing policy in 983. He established a National University (Gukjagam), introduced the Chinese civil service examination system to select officials, and promoted the Confucian classics. Seongjong's reforms had a profound impact on Goryeo's political system and the spread of Confucian culture in Korea.

v.t: Venice obtains commercial privileges from Byzantium
v.s: In return for Venetian naval support, Byzantine Emperor Basil II granted Venetian merchants duty-free trading privileges in Constantinople. Venice began to dominate eastern Mediterranean trade.

v.t: Emperor Taizong of Song changes reign title to Yongxi
v.s: In the ninth year of the Taiping Xingguo era (984 CE), Emperor Taizong of Song changed the reign title to Yongxi. During the Yongxi era (984–987), the Song continued its unification campaigns. In 985, the Song launched the Yongxi Northern Campaign against the Liao, hoping to recover the Sixteen Prefectures of Yanyun, but it ended in a disastrous defeat at the Battle of Gaoliang River.

v.t: Holy Roman Emperor Otto III is a minor
v.s: In 984 CE, the three-year-old Otto III became Holy Roman Emperor. Due to his youth, the empire was governed by his mother, the Byzantine princess Theophanu, and his grandmother Adelaide. During Otto III's childhood, the empire's borders were troubled by Slavic and Danish incursions.

v.t: Spanish Christian kingdom recovers Coimbra
v.s: In 984 CE, the army of the Kingdom of León in Spain recaptured Coimbra (in modern-day central Portugal) from the Moors. Coimbra became an important city of León/Portugal and a forward base of the Reconquista.

v.t: Persian poet Ferdowsi completes the Shahnameh
v.s: After more than thirty years of composition, Ferdowsi completed the Shahnameh, the Persian national epic, consisting of sixty thousand couplets and recounting Persian myths and the lives of its kings. This monumental work saved the Persian language and culture.

v.t: Song-Liao war resumes
v.s: Emperor Taizong of Song decided to take the offensive against the Khitans, launching a three-pronged northern campaign. The eastern force under Cao Bin left Xiongzhou, the central force under Tian Chongjin left Feihu, and the western force under Pan Mei and Yang Ye left Yanmen. They captured several prefectures in the initial stages.

v.t: Chola Empire begins to expand
v.s: Rajaraja I, king of the Chola dynasty in South India, ascended the throne. He built a powerful navy and began expanding into Sri Lanka and the Maldives, marking the start of the Chola Empire's peak.

v.t: Li Jiqian submits to the Liao
v.s: In 985 CE, the Tangut chief Li Jiqian, to oppose the Northern Song's military pressure, surrendered to the Liao. Emperor Shengzong of Liao conferred on Li Jiqian the title of "King of Xia" and provided military support. Li Jiqian exploited the Song-Liao conflict to develop his power, laying the foundation for the later Western Xia.

v.t: King Æthelred II of England comes of age
v.s: In 985 CE, King Æthelred II the Unready of Wessex came of age (about 18) and began to rule personally. Æthelred's reign is notable for his continuous wars with Danish Vikings; he tried to buy peace by paying Danegeld, but this failed.

v.t: Battle of Qigou Pass: the Song army is routed
v.s: The Song's three-pronged northern campaign met the main Khitan army at Qigou Pass (present-day Zhuozhou, Hebei). Empress Dowager Xiao Chuo led the Khitan army in person; Yelü Xiuge cut off the Song's supply lines. The eastern force under Cao Bin collapsed, suffering tens of thousands of casualties. Emperor Taizong ordered a general retreat.

v.t: Yang Ye is captured and starves himself to death
v.s: The Song general Yang Ye (Yang Jive), while covering the retreat of civilians, was surrounded by Khitan forces at Chenjia Valley. Wounded and captured, he died after fasting for three days. He is the prototype of the legendary "Yang family generals."

v.t: Byzantines conquer Bulgaria
v.s: Byzantine Emperor Basil II launched a large-scale war against Bulgaria, capturing northern Bulgarian fortresses. Tsar Samuel of Bulgaria was forced to withdraw to the mountains.

v.t: The Eiga Monogatari is compiled in Japan
v.s: The Japanese noblewoman Akazome Emon began writing the Eiga Monogatari (Tale of Flowering Fortunes), recording the rise of Fujiwara no Michinaga's family. It is the first historical work written by a Japanese noblewoman.

v.t: Northern Song shifts to strategic defense against Liao
v.s: After the disastrous defeat at Qigou Pass, Emperor Taizong abandoned northern campaigns and instead fortified the border. He ordered border commanders not to provoke the Khitans and built up the military strongholds of Dingzhou and Zhenzhou. The Song and Liao entered a stalemate.

v.t: Toltecs occupy Chichen Itza
v.s: Huemac, chief of the Toltecs from central Mexico, led an army to conquer the Maya city-state of Chichen Itza in Yucatán. The blending of Toltec and Maya cultures created the Maya-Toltec civilization.

v.t: Capetian dynasty founded
v.s: King Louis V of West Francia died without issue. Hugh Capet, Count of Paris, was elected king by an assembly of nobles, founding the Capetian dynasty. Initially, the Capetians controlled only a small territory around Paris, but the dynasty lasted more than three hundred years.

v.t: Mahmud of Ghazni invades India
v.s: Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni led an army to India, capturing Peshawar. He plundered the treasuries of Indian temples, destroyed Buddhist monasteries, and introduced Islam to northern India.

v.t: Baptism of Kievan Rus'
v.s: After his baptism at Chersonesus, Grand Prince Vladimir of Kiev returned to Kiev and ordered the entire populace to be baptized in the Dnieper River. He also built Orthodox churches throughout the country and hired Byzantine priests to preach. The Rus' thus entered the Christian world.

v.t: King Sweyn I of Denmark ascends the throne
v.s: King Harald Bluetooth of Denmark was overthrown in a coup by his son Sweyn I Forkbeard. Sweyn I continued to promote Christianity and prepared a major invasion of England.

v.t: King Olof Skötkonung of Sweden converts to Christianity
v.s: King Olof Skötkonung of Sweden was baptized at Uppsala, becoming the first Christian king of Sweden. Christianity spread further in Scandinavia.

v.t: Emperor Taizong of Song establishes the "Fengzhuang Ku" to reserve military funds
v.s: In preparation for a future northern campaign, Emperor Taizong set up the Fengzhuang Ku (Sealed Treasury), depositing budget surpluses there exclusively for the recovery of the Sixteen Prefectures. However, the treasury was never used during his reign.

v.t: Emperor Taizong of Song issues an edict seeking frank advice
v.s: Due to frequent natural disasters, Emperor Taizong issued an edict seeking frank advice, encouraging officials and commoners to write letters criticizing government policies. The official Wang Yucheng submitted a memorial, "Duangong Admonition," which criticized problems in the administration, and the emperor accepted it.

v.t: Song-Liao conflict escalates: Battle of Yizhou
v.s: A large Liao army advanced south and besieged Yizhou (present-day Yi County, Hebei). The Song general Yuan Jizhong defended the city; the Liao army failed to take it. After receiving reinforcements, Empress Dowager Xiao Chuo captured Yizhou, and the Song army withdrew to Mancheng.

v.t: Fujiwara no Michinaga is born in Japan
v.s: In 989 CE (Eien 3), Fujiwara no Michinaga was born. He became the most powerful politician of the Heian period, the architect of the Fujiwara regency's golden age. Michinaga married four of his daughters to emperors, making the Fujiwara the maternal relatives of the emperor and thus controlling the court.

v.t: Byzantine Basil II establishes military districts (themata)
v.s: Byzantine Emperor Basil II reformed the thema system, confiscating the estates of large landowners in Asia Minor and distributing them to soldiers in service. This strengthened the Byzantine military.

v.t: Li Jiqian is enfeoffed as King of Xia by the Liao
v.s: Emperor Shengzong of Liao formally invested Li Jiqian as "King of Xia" and bestowed upon him Khitan armor and horses. With Liao backing, Li Jiqian consolidated his position in Xiazhou and constantly raided the Song's northwestern border.

v.t: Toltec capital of Tula flourishes
v.s: Tula, the capital of the Toltecs (in present-day Hidalgo State, Mexico), reached its zenith. The city featured warrior statues, pyramids, and ballcourts; Toltec culture influenced the later Aztec civilization.

v.t: Kievan Rus' and Byzantium form a marriage alliance
v.s: Grand Prince Vladimir of Kiev married Anna, the sister of Byzantine Emperor Basil II. The alliance strengthened Rus'-Byzantine ties and reinforced Orthodoxy in Rus'.

v.t: Oghuz Yabgu State, precursor to Kazakh Khanate, takes shape
v.s: Around 990 CE, the Oghuz Turks on the Central Asian steppe established the Yabgu State. The Oghuz Yabgu State was a predecessor of the Kazakh Khanate, controlling trade routes across the steppe. The Oghuz began converting to Islam in the late 10th century.

v.t: Wang Yucheng submits the "Duangong Admonition"
v.s: In the second year of the Chunhua era (991 CE), the Song official Wang Yucheng submitted the "Duangong Admonition" to Emperor Taizong. In this memorial, Wang frankly criticized government problems, including bureaucratic corruption and weak border defenses. Although the emperor appreciated his courage, he did not actually adopt reform measures. Wang Yucheng was noted for his outspoken remonstrances.

v.t: Feudal structure of the Holy Roman Empire solidifies
v.s: After Otto III came to power, he further strengthened the hierarchical feudal structure of the empire: ducal territories became hereditary, while the emperor's direct domain shrank. The empire gradually moved toward a loose federation.

v.t: Irish king Brian Boru unites Ireland
v.s: Brian Boru defeated the Dublin Vikings and united all of Ireland. He styled himself "Emperor of the Irish" and is considered one of Ireland's greatest monarchs.

v.t: Karakhanids capture Kashgar
v.s: In 991 CE (actually about 1006), Karakhanid forces captured Kashgar (in modern Xinjiang). Kashgar had been an important Buddhist center of the Uyghurs. Its capture by the Karakhanids promoted the Islamization of the region. Kashgar became a major city of the Karakhanid state.

v.t: Bolesław I becomes Duke of Poland
v.s: Duke Mieszko I of Poland died and was succeeded by his son Bolesław I the Brave. He unified the Polish tribes and established an independent Polish province, known as the father of Poland.

v.t: War breaks out between Goryeo and Liao
v.s: Emperor Shengzong of Liao, on the pretext that Goryeo lacked submission, led a large army to invade Goryeo. King Seongjong of Goryeo sued for peace, and the Liao army withdrew. Goryeo accepted Liao's investiture, broke off formal tribute relations with the Song, and became a vassal of the Liao.

v.t: Emperor Taizong of Song reforms the official system
v.s: In the third year of the Chunhua era (992 CE), Emperor Taizong of Song reformed the central government. He refined the systems of the Zhongshu Sheng (Central Secretariat) and the Shumiyuan (Privy Council), clarifying the division of powers between the chancellor and the privy councilors. He also reduced some overlapping institutions. These reforms strengthened centralization and administrative efficiency.

v.t: University of Córdoba is expanded
v.s: In 992 CE, the Caliphate of Córdoba (the Umayyads of Spain) expanded the University of Córdoba in the capital. The university was one of the most renowned academic centers in Europe at the time, offering studies in medicine, astronomy, mathematics, and philosophy. Scholars from all over Europe came to study there.

v.t: Wang Xiaobo and Li Shun revolt breaks out
v.s: A peasant by the name of Wang Xiaobo in Qingcheng County, Sichuan, launched a rebellion with the slogan "equalize the rich and poor." He captured Qingcheng and Pengshan, and his army quickly swelled to tens of thousands. After Wang was killed in battle, Li Shun took over, captured Chengdu, and established the Great Shu regime.

v.t: Chola Empire invades Sri Lanka
v.s: The South Indian Chola king Rajaraja I dispatched a powerful navy across the Palk Strait to capture Anuradhapura in northern Sri Lanka. The Chola Empire made northern Ceylon a province, ruling for about half a century.

v.t: Byzantine educational revival
v.s: Byzantine Emperor Basil II promoted education, restoring the University of Constantinople and hiring distinguished scholars to teach philosophy, rhetoric, and mathematics. Classical Greek works were systematically organized and commented upon.

v.t: The Pillow Book (Makura no Sōshi) is written in Japan
v.s: Around 993 CE (Shōryaku 4), Sei Shōnagon began writing her essay collection Makura no Sōshi (The Pillow Book). Sei Shōnagon was a female author of the Heian period who served as a lady-in-waiting to Empress Teishi (Sadako), consort of Emperor Ichijō. The Pillow Book recorded in exquisite detail various aspects of court life.

v.t: Northern Song suppresses the Li Shun rebellion
v.s: Emperor Taizong of Song sent the eunuch Wang Ji'en with a large army into Sichuan to suppress the Li Shun rebellion. Government forces stormed Chengdu, and Li Shun was killed. Although the rebellion failed, its slogan "equalize the rich and poor" had a lasting impact. Afterwards, Emperor Taizong adopted pacification measures in Sichuan, reducing taxes and punishing corrupt officials.

v.t: Fatimid Caliphate at its zenith
v.s: Under Caliph al-Aziz, the Fatimid Caliphate reached its peak, with territory including North Africa, Sicily, Syria, and the Hejaz. Cairo became an Islamic cultural and scholarly center, with Al-Azhar University attracting scholars from far and wide.

v.t: Bolesław I of Poland conquers Silesia
v.s: Bolesław I the Brave led his army to conquer Silesia (upper Oder River region), expanding his territory to the Elbe. He subsequently came into conflict with the Holy Roman Empire.

v.t: Otto III is crowned Holy Roman Emperor
v.s: Otto III, at age sixteen, was crowned Holy Roman Emperor by Pope Gregory V in Rome. He aimed to revive the ancient Roman Empire, making Rome his capital.

v.t: Emperor Taizong of Song appoints Zhao Heng as crown prince, stabilizing the succession
v.s: Emperor Taizong of Song formally appointed his third son Zhao Heng (later Emperor Zhenzong) as crown prince. The question of succession had been unresolved because the eldest son, Zhao Yuanzuo, was considered insane and was deposed, and the second son, Zhao Yuanxi, had died young. Zhao Heng was kind-hearted and studious, and received the support of important ministers such as Chancellor Lü Duan. Establishing the crown prince stabilized the political situation and laid the foundation for a peaceful succession after the emperor's death.

v.t: Northern Song strengthens governance of Sichuan after Li Shun rebellion
v.s: The Song court dispatched the eunuch Wang Ji'en with a large army to Sichuan, where he stormed Chengdu and suppressed the Great Shu regime. Li Shun's whereabouts after the fall of the city are unknown (some say he was killed). The court then carried out extensive military mopping-up operations and relief measures in Sichuan. The "equalize the rich and poor" policy of the rebels was abandoned, but some of their suggestions were adopted, such as reducing tax burdens on poor peasants. Although the rebellion failed, its slogan deeply influenced later generations.

v.t: Olaf I of Norway forcefully promotes Christianity
v.s: King Olaf I of Norway, returning from England, used force to impose Christianity. He ordered the destruction of statues of Norse gods, built churches throughout the country, and imported English clergy. Nobles and peasants who refused baptism were exiled or executed. Olaf I also promoted Christianity in Iceland and Greenland. Although he was killed at the Battle of Svolder in 1000, the foundations of Christianity in Norway had been firmly laid.

v.t: Mahmud of Ghazni begins his plundering campaigns in northern India
v.s: Having consolidated his rule in Afghanistan, Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni began systematic plundering of northern India. He led Turkic cavalry through the Khyber Pass, capturing cities such as Peshawar from Hindu kingdoms, seizing vast amounts of gold, silver, and slaves, and plundering Hindu temples. Mahmud's expeditions opened the way for Islam to enter northern India, but also destroyed numerous Buddhist and Hindu monasteries.

v.t: Grand Prince Vladimir of Kiev builds defensive fortifications
v.s: To defend against incursions by the Pecheneg nomads, Grand Prince Vladimir I of Kiev built a series of fortifications and a wall on the left bank of the Dnieper. He recruited warriors from the north to garrison the frontier and established a signal-fire system. These projects strengthened the southern border defense of Kievan Rus', ensuring the security of Kiev and allowing the Rus' economy and culture to develop peacefully.

v.t: Fujiwara no Michinaga becomes Minister of the Interior, rising to absolute power
v.s: The Japanese noble Fujiwara no Michinaga was appointed Minister of the Interior (naidaijin), becoming the most powerful figure in the court. By placing his daughters in the palace as consorts of the emperor, he gradually took control of government. Michinaga later rose to the rank of chancellor (kampaku), achieving the position of "three empresses from one family" and bringing the Fujiwara regency to its peak. His glorious life is recorded in the Eiga Monogatari, and the Heian aristocratic culture reached its zenith during this time.

v.t: King Mokjong of Goryeo implements land reform
v.s: During the reign of King Mokjong of Goryeo, supplementary regulations to the jeonsigwa (land and forest classification) system were issued, stipulating that land and forests should be distributed according to official rank to limit aristocratic land accumulation. He also established granaries to regulate grain prices and provide famine relief. These measures eased social tensions but did not solve the problem of land concentration.

v.t: Expansion of Al-Azhar University in Cairo
v.s: Fatimid Caliph al-Aziz ordered the expansion of the Al-Azhar Mosque in Cairo, adding a library and lecture halls, formally establishing an Islamic institution of higher learning. Al-Azhar University taught jurisprudence, grammar, logic, astronomy, and medicine, attracting students from throughout the Islamic world and becoming a lasting academic center.

v.t: Li Jiqian captures Lingzhou, rise of Western Xia
v.s: The Tangut leader Li Jiqian led his army to capture Lingzhou (present-day Lingwu, Ningxia), a major Song stronghold in the northwest, and renamed it Xipingfu, making it an early political center of the Western Xia. The loss of Lingzhou cut off the Song's communication with the Hexi Corridor, and Western Xia expanded rapidly. Song control over the northwest never recovered.

v.t: Byzantines build border fortresses
v.s: Byzantine Emperor Basil II ordered the construction of a large number of small stone forts (krepitonia) in eastern Anatolia and the Balkans. These forts, each with a garrison, granary, and signal tower, effectively defended against Arab and Bulgarian incursions, consolidating the imperial borders.

v.t: Otto III invades Poland
v.s: Holy Roman Emperor Otto III led an army to invade Poland, forcing Duke Bolesław I the Brave to submit. Otto established an archbishopric at Gniezno, attempting to incorporate Poland into the imperial church system.

v.t: Portions of The Tale of Genji begin to circulate in Japan
v.s: The Heian-period female author Murasaki Shikibu began to present chapters of The Tale of Genji (Genji Monogatari) at court, causing a sensation. This novel, the world's first, with its delicate psychological descriptions and elegant language, established a high point of Japanese classical literature.

v.t: Emperor Taizong of Song dies; Emperor Zhenzong succeeds
v.s: Emperor Taizong of Song died of illness in Kaifeng at the age of fifty-eight. His third son, Zhao Heng, succeeded him as Emperor Zhenzong. After his accession, Zhenzong changed the reign title to Xianping and initially worked hard to govern, employing virtuous ministers and reducing taxes. This period is known as the "Xianping Restoration," a rare time of peace and prosperity in the early Northern Song.

v.t: The Granada region flourishes
v.s: The city of Granada in southern Spain, under the rule of the Umayyad caliphs, became prosperous. Situated at the foot of the Sierra Nevada, it had developed irrigated agriculture and was an important commercial and cultural center of the Caliphate of Córdoba. During the Nasrid dynasty (1232–1492), Granada became the last Islamic kingdom in Spain, and the Alhambra is its most magnificent architectural legacy.

v.t: Sweyn I of Denmark conquers England
v.s: King Sweyn I of Denmark (Sweyn Forkbeard) led a huge Viking fleet to invade England, capturing London and forcing King Æthelred II the Unready to flee to Normandy. Sweyn I proclaimed himself king of England, and the Danish empire reached its peak.

v.t: King Mokjong of Goryeo executes a powerful minister
v.s: Early in the reign of King Mokjong of Goryeo (r. 997–1009), he executed an arrogant, powerful minister. Mokjong tried to strengthen royal power, but the struggle between the throne and the aristocratic families continued. In 1009, Mokjong was killed in a coup, and the Goryeo dynasty fell into new turmoil.

v.t: Emperor Zhenzong changes reign title to Xianping, beginning the Xianping Restoration
v.s: Emperor Zhenzong of Song changed the reign title to Xianping (meaning "universal peace"). In the early part of his reign, he was diligent in government affairs, frugal in his personal life, reduced taxes, and promoted production. He appointed virtuous chancellors such as Li Hang and Kou Zhun, and improved the efficiency of the bureaucracy. This period is known as the "Xianping Restoration," a rare era of good governance in the early Northern Song.

v.t: Construction of the Kukulcán Pyramid at Chichen Itza
v.s: Under Toltec influence, the city-state of Chichen Itza began construction of the Kukulcán Pyramid (El Castillo). The pyramid stands about thirty meters high, with ninety-one steps on each of its four sides; including the top platform, the total number of steps is 365, matching the number of days in the solar year, demonstrating the Maya's outstanding achievements in astronomy and architecture.

v.t: Otto III moves his court to Rome
v.s: To fulfill his dream of reviving the ancient Roman Empire, Holy Roman Emperor Otto III moved his court from Aachen to Rome. He installed his former teacher Gerbert as pope (Sylvester II), attempting to create a universal Christian empire centered on Rome.

v.t: Persian philosopher Ibn Sina (Avicenna) is born
v.s: The Persian philosopher and physician Ibn Sina (Avicenna) was born near Bukhara. He later became one of the most outstanding scholars of the Islamic Golden Age, authoring The Canon of Medicine and The Book of Healing, which profoundly influenced the European Renaissance.

v.t: Otto III campaigns in Italy
v.s: Holy Roman Emperor Otto III launched a military campaign in Italy to suppress a rebellion of Roman nobles. He attempted to restore the territory of the Roman Empire and competed with the Byzantine Empire for control of southern Italy. Otto also planned to marry a Byzantine princess to unite the Christian East and West, but the plan was cut short by his early death.

v.t: Millennial panic in Europe
v.s: Widespread fear of the millennium spread throughout Western Christendom. Many believers thought the year 1000, or 1033 (the thousandth anniversary of Christ's crucifixion), would be the end of the world. People sold their property, undertook pilgrimages to Jerusalem, and built countless churches in an effort to appease God. However, the world did not end; instead, Europe entered the High Middle Ages.

v.t: Battle of Yingzhou (Song-Liao)
v.s: Emperor Shengzong of Liao led a large army south against the Song, fighting a fierce battle at Yingzhou (present-day Hejian, Hebei). The Song general Fan Tingzhao defended the city and used artillery to repel the Liao army. After a long siege failed, the Liao army withdrew, and the Song successfully defended the border. The battle showed the growing strength of the Song border defenses.

v.t: Construction of Saint Sophia Cathedral in Kiev
v.s: Grand Prince Vladimir of Kiev ordered the construction of Saint Sophia Cathedral in Kiev, modeled on the eponymous cathedral in Constantinople. This Orthodox cathedral, built in Byzantine style, was a symbol of the Christianization of Rus'.

v.t: The Tale of Genji is completed
v.s: The Heian-period Japanese female author Murasaki Shikibu completed The Tale of Genji, the world's first novel. The work consists of fifty-four chapters and tells the story of the aristocratic Hikaru Genji, covering his love affairs and political career, and vividly depicting the various aspects of court life in the Heian period. Its elegant language and delicate emotional insight have made it the supreme masterpiece of Japanese classical literature, with a profound influence on later writers.

v.t: Leif Erikson reaches North America
v.s: The Norse Viking explorer Leif Erikson led a crew of about 35 from Greenland westward and reached the coast of North America in the area of Newfoundland (which he called Vinland). Archaeological evidence at L'Anse aux Meadows in Newfoundland has confirmed a Viking settlement, proving that Vikings were the first Europeans to reach America, nearly five hundred years before Columbus.

v.t: Seljuk Turk Empire flourishes
v.s: The Seljuk Turks rose in Central Asia and established a vast empire. The Seljuks were a branch of the Oghuz Turks; their leader Seljuk and his successors conquered most of Central Asia and eastern Persia. The Seljuks converted to Sunni Islam, and in 1055 they captured Baghdad, replacing the Buwayhids as the protectors of the Abbasid caliphs. The Seljuk Empire had a profound impact on the Crusades and the political landscape of the medieval Islamic world.

v.t: Porcelain technology matures in Jingdezhen
v.s: During the Northern Song period, Chinese porcelain-making technology reached a high level of maturity. Kilns in Jingdezhen and other places produced large quantities of fine white and celadon wares, especially qingbai (yingqing) porcelain. Song porcelain was renowned for its elegant shapes and warm glaze colors and became a popular export along the Maritime Silk Road to Southeast Asia, South Asia, the Middle East, and East Africa.

v.t: Chichen Itza becomes a major power in Yucatán
v.s: The Maya city-state of Chichen Itza rose to become the dominant political entity in the Yucatán Peninsula. The city blended indigenous Maya and Toltec cultural elements; its iconic structures—the Kukulcán Pyramid, the observatory (El Caracol), and the Temple of the Warriors—display a distinctive architectural style. Chichen Itza ruled the northern Yucatán during the Postclassic period (c. 900–1200), becoming one of the most prosperous and powerful city-states in Mesoamerica.

v.t: Otto III's Roman imperial dream
v.s: Holy Roman Emperor Otto III, who had been educated in the classical tradition and was deeply influenced by Byzantine culture and Roman antiquities, carried out a series of political and religious activities aimed at reviving the ancient Roman Empire. He moved his court to Rome, appointed his former teacher Sylvester II as pope, and attempted to create a universal Christian empire centered on Rome. However, Otto III died of malaria in Italy in 1002 at the age of only twenty-one, and his imperial revival plan collapsed with him.

v.t: Normans begin to enter southern Italy
v.s: Norsemen (Normans) began to play an important role in Mediterranean affairs. The Normans, originally a branch of the Vikings, had settled in Normandy, France, in the early 10th century. In the early 11th century, Norman adventurers traveled south to Italy, where they served as mercenaries for Lombard and Byzantine lords. Robert Guiscard of the Hauteville family and others gradually established their own power, eventually founding the Norman Kingdom of Sicily.

v.t: Sufism spreads widely in the Islamic world
v.s: Sufism, the mystical branch of Islam, spread widely across the Islamic world. Sufism emphasizes direct experience of the divine through meditation, prayer, and ritual, seeking inner purification and union with God. Famous Sufi masters such as al-Hallaj and al-Ghazali made important contributions to Sufi thought. Sufism played a significant role in the spread of Islam, especially in Central Asia, South Asia, and Africa.

v.t: Finnish tribes settle
v.s: The Finnic tribes (including the Suomi, Hämäläiset, and Karjalaiset) formed relatively stable social organizations in the region of Finland. The Finns practiced traditional shamanism, lived by fishing, hunting, gathering, and primitive agriculture. Before the Swedish crusades (12th–13th centuries), Finland had not yet formed a unified state, and each tribe maintained its own autonomy and traditions.

v.t: Spread of Buddhism in the Liao dynasty
v.s: During the Liao dynasty, Buddhism spread widely among the Khitans. Most Liao emperors were devout Buddhists; they built numerous monasteries and pagodas in the capital Shangjing Linhuangfu and Nanjing Xijin (present-day Beijing). Liao Buddhism was dominated by Huayan and Esoteric Buddhism; the Liao also printed the famous Khitan Tripitaka. Buddhism had a profound impact on Liao politics, culture, architecture, and daily life.

v.t: Polynesians settle Easter Island
v.s: Polynesian navigators reached Easter Island (Rapa Nui), one of the most remote islands on Earth, and settled there. Over the following centuries, they built hundreds of gigantic stone statues (moai), weighing about eighty tons and standing up to ten meters tall. These statues are a marvel of ancient Polynesian civilization; their purpose and method of transport remain a mystery.

v.t: Formation of Italian pasta tradition
v.s: The Italian tradition of pasta-making began to take shape. While similar noodle-like foods existed in ancient Rome, it is believed that true pasta was introduced to Sicily by Arabs in the early Middle Ages—they brought the technique of drying noodles. Over time, various regions of Italy developed their own pasta shapes and cooking methods, and pasta became one of the most iconic elements of Italian cuisine.

v.t: Border conflicts continue between Song Zhenzong and Liao
v.s: After Emperor Zhenzong of Song ascended the throne, the Liao continued to provoke along the border. Both sides engaged in numerous small-scale clashes in Hebei and Shanxi, with victories on both sides. The Song adopted a defensive strategy, strengthening border fortifications and training troops, preparing for the eventual Shanyuan Treaty.

v.t: Ancient Hawaiian chiefdom society forms
v.s: Around 1000 CE, Polynesian voyagers established complex chiefdom societies in the Hawaiian Islands. Hawaiians developed large-scale terraced irrigation systems for taro cultivation, constructed fishponds, and built temple platforms (heiau). Society was strictly stratified into chiefs (aliʻi), priests (kahuna), and commoners. Oral traditions such as the creation chant Kumulipo preserved genealogies and history for centuries. In the late 18th century, Kamehameha I first unified the Hawaiian Islands.

v.t: Early Micronesian and Polynesian navigation networks
v.s: Around 1000 BCE, Austronesian voyagers completed the initial colonization of Micronesia and western Polynesia. Tonga and Samoa became the cradle of Polynesian culture and language (the "Polynesian homeland"). Tongans developed a complex chiefdom system and built large stone structures (e.g., the Haʻamonga ʻa Maui trilithon on Tongatapu). Around 1000 CE, Polynesians from these islands went on to colonize the Marquesas, the Society Islands, Hawaii, Easter Island, and New Zealand.

v.t: Chinese sericulture (silk farming) technology reaches Europe
v.s: Between about 550 and 1000 CE, Chinese sericulture and silk-reeling techniques slowly spread westward along the Silk Road. Around 550 CE, two Nestorian monks smuggled silkworm eggs to the Byzantine Empire by hiding them in hollow bamboo staves. Thereafter, sericulture developed in southern Europe (Greece, Italy, Spain). By the 11th century, northern Italian cities such as Lucca, Florence, and Venice became famous for their silk textiles.

v.t: Chinese Jiaozi: the world's first paper money
v.s: In the early 11th century, wealthy merchants in Sichuan, China, because iron coins were heavy and inconvenient to carry, began to issue private promissory notes called jiaozi—the world's first paper money. In 1023, the Song government established the Jiaozi Office in Yizhou (Chengdu) and took over the issuance of jiaozi. The invention of paper money was a revolution in monetary history, enabling large-scale trade without the need to transport heavy metal coins. However, subsequent Song, Jin, and Yuan dynasties all suffered hyperinflation from over-issuance; a stable paper currency system required strict fiscal discipline.
